I'll work under the assumption that you already know it, but to be sure, I want to clarify that the dependency would _only_ affect Python 2.6. It would not be installed for any other version.

What I'm thinking, then, is that `InlineTableDict` will end up being more of an abstract base class (not really, since it'll be fine for 3rd parties to use it directly, but we won't), and we'll use a dynamically created subclass of both `_dict` and `InlineTableDict` for inline tables. `InlineTableDict` needs to be a public interface, or else the power of `InlineTableDict` is only available to this library internally, which I think is less than ideal. It'll be a bit of a messy change, but it's certainly doable.

I'll pull out the uncontroversial fixup for `InlineTableDict.__init__` method into a separate pull request.

From the spec:

> For writing long strings without introducing extraneous whitespace, use a "line ending backslash". When the last non-whitespace character on a line is a `\`, it will be trimmed along with all whitespace (including newlines) up to the next non-whitespace character or closing delimiter.

Closing as WONTFIX due to compliance with the spec.

candidate_groups is the line split on commas which is a list. candidate_group is the current group being parsed/built up which is a string. I think the re-written code should be something like this:

candidate_groups[0] = candidate_group + "," + candidate_groups[0]

- load_inline_object doesn't have a return statement but its return value is used in a conditional in loads. Is this intentional? Why?
- I think the checks on the status to continue are unnecessary since those `continue` statements are the last statements in those loops

I think everything else is OK.

Hi @mfeif 

Currently, Plim does not have an API for extending the default markdown parser and, as a quick solution, I can suggest writing your own [custom extension](http://plim.readthedocs.org/en/latest/en/extensions.html#extending-plim-with-custom-parsers). In your example, it would look like this:

```python
import re
import markdown2
from plim import lexer, preprocessor_factory

SMARTYMARK_EXTRAS = ["smartypants"]
SMARTYMARK_BLOCK_RE = re.compile('-\s*(?P<section>smartymark)\s*')

# Please refer to source docs with regard to parser's API:
# https://github.com/avanov/Plim/blob/0.9.10/plim/lexer.py#L141
def parse_smartymark(indent_level, current_line, matched, source, syntax):
    parsed_block, tail_indent, tail_line, source = lexer.parse_explicit_literal_no_embedded(
        indent_level,
        lexer.LITERAL_CONTENT_PREFIX,
        matched,
        source,
        syntax
    )
    parsed_block = markdown2.markdown(parsed_block, extras=SMARTYMARK_EXTRAS)
    return parsed_block.strip(), tail_indent, tail_line, source

MY_PARSERS = [
    (SMARTYMARK_BLOCK_RE, parse_smartymark),
]

# Create a custom Plim preprocessor that can be fed to Mako API
preprocessor = preprocessor_factory(custom_parsers=MY_PARSERS, syntax='mako')
```
Once you have a custom preprocessor with your own extensions, you can use it instead of the default one.

Personally, I prefer custom preprocessors to API extensions, though you may find this approach cumbersome. Therefore, feel free to suggest an API extension as you'd like to see it. I can't promise to accept them, but at least we could discuss it and review possible benefits.

最后再说明一下问题 #103 中涉及到的 `.idx`, `.edx`, `.ntc`, `.etc`的文件格式:

* `.ntc`, `.etc`: 这些是某只股票或者基金的分笔数据文件，数据是按照顺序追加到文件中的。
> `.etc`: 文件是基金数据文件，数据没有经过加密，格式如下:
```
00000000 TickTradeDetail struc ; (sizeof=0x10)
00000000 dateTime        dw ?                    ; 开市后的分钟数，通过 SetTradeTime() 转换。
00000002 price           dd ?                    ; 成交价格,需要再处理一下
00000006 volume          dd ?                    ; 成交
0000000A count           dd ?                    ; 笔数
0000000E type            dw ?                    ; 0=buy;1=sell;2=unknown
00000010 TickTradeDetail ends
```
> `.ntc`: 文件是股票数据文件，数据是有加密的，结构如下:
```
00000000 TickDetail      struc ; (sizeof=0x8)
00000000 type            dd ?                    ; base 16
00000004 unknown2        dd ?                    ; base 16
00000008 TickDetail      ends
00000008
00000000 ; ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
00000000
00000000 TickItem        struc ; (sizeof=0x14, variable size)
00000000 dateTime        dd ?
00000004 count           dw ?
00000006 volumeOffset    dw ?                    ; base 16
00000008 volumeSize      dw ?                    ; base 16
0000000A type            dw ?
0000000C price           dd ?
00000010 volume          dd ?
00000014 tickDetail      db 0 dup(?)             ; struct  offset
00000014 TickItem        ends
```

* `.idx`, `.edx`: 这些是索引文件。是通达信同于管理这些分笔数据的索引文件。
> 由于用户保存分笔数据无法绝对按照顺序保存，所以通过索引文件来定位某天的分笔数据在数据文件中的偏移。

索引文件结构是统一的，格式如下:
```
typedef struct {
    int  itemDate;     // 指定的日期
    WORD size;         // 日期对应的逐笔数据大小
    DWORD unknown      <format=hex>;     // 
} DateNode;

typedef struct {
    int startDate;     // 分笔数据的开始日期
    int endDate;       // 分笔数据的结束日期
    WORD dayCount;     // 天数
    DateNode items[dayCount];
} TICK_IDX;
```

附件上传索引文件的解析格式: 
[TdxIdxFile.bt.txt](https://github.com/rainx/pytdx/files/1821517/TdxIdxFile.bt.txt)

@dabeaz:
> Full disclosure: without measuring it, I have no idea what polling for I/O on every task schedule would do, but I have a hard time believing that it would be free for the last scenario described

It's an interesting question, and probably worth measuring at some point just to know. For systems where we use a modern I/O API (epoll on Linux, kqueue on MacOS/*BSD, hopefully someday IOCP on Windows), then polling on every task schedule might turn out to be not free, but very cheap -- it should basically be as cheap as doing a cheap syscall. OTOH right now we wouldn't really get any benefit from doing this either.

@ZhukovAlexander:
> The last thing I want to get a resolution for is throttling. Do you guys think this is a valuable idea that deserves a dedicated PR?

I still don't understand how it could help, but if you put together a proof-of-concept demonstrating that it provides a nice win on some benchmark then I guess that'd be pretty convincing?

@dabeaz 
> the core Curio kernel should remain basically the same as it is now aside from bug fixes and cleanup--meaning that it's simple and fast. If someone wants to play around with different scheduling policies, it should be done by creating custom code that lives outside the kernel. Make a custom Socket object or something.

FWIW, there are two possible extensions to the current scheduling model that I've been thinking might eventually make sense to add to the core Curio kernel:

* "strict" priorities: i.e., tasks with low priorities don't run at all while there are higher-priority tasks runnable (like Linux SCHED_FIFO priorities). This is a somewhat common feature in other event loops ([example](https://developer.gnome.org/glib/stable/glib-The-Main-Event-Loop.html#G-PRIORITY-DEFAULT-IDLE:CAPS), [example](https://www.w3.org/TR/requestidlecallback/)), and useful for things like "I only want this background task to run if nothing else is going on", or "I want this interactive debugging tool to always take priority, even if the server is otherwise melting down trying to handle way too many incoming connections".

* weighted fair queueing: i.e., "optimally fair" scheuling in the sense that tasks that hold onto the event loop longer before yielding get scheduled less often, plus "soft" priorities that can be used to modulate this (like Unix "nice" values). The implementation for this is simple, fast, and elegant, and it might make curio-based programs less janky under load in [cases like this one](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40601119/python-ssh-server-twisted-conch-takes-up-high-cpu-usage-when-a-large-number-of/40627446). Or it might not be useful at all -- I can't find any prior art for WFQ + cooperative scheduling. But worth experimenting with.

These are absolutely things where the next step is "play around with them outside of curio proper", and there are plenty of higher priority (heh :-)) things to worry about. But I mention them here because AFAICT they can't be implemented without modifying the kernel itself and otherwise integrating with core curio APIs. Which is fine, it just means that in the short term the method for playing around with them is to make a branch instead of writing a custom `Socket` object.

Let me elaborate just to add to the discussion. I think that it might be very confusing because we might have to put rules regarding spacing. We could change the precedence rules and say that units to the right of a number are one element. In this case: what next would mean (together, separated by space(s), by `*`)? Is always clear the intention of the user?

For example:

- `600mg/(24hours)` => `25 mg / hour` 
No issue here.
- `600mg/24hours` => `25 mg / hour`
Intuitively I would say `24hours` is one element.
- `600mg/24 hours`  => ?
Because hours is a unit, you could say `24 hours` is one element and the output should be `25 mg / hour`. However, following the easy to remember rule that units are exactly like numbers, the result is 25 mg * hour.
- `600mg/24*hours`  => ?
Same problem here. What is the intention of the user? 

If the last means 25 mg * hour, does it make sense that `600mg/24hours`, `600mg/24 hours` and `600mg/24*hours` give different results?

The approach in Pint is that we should not guess user intention because it might lead to subtle bugs.

Currently there's no support for extracting the sequence from a gff file. In this case you would manually extract the sequence into a separate fasta file. Once you have that, the `Feature.sequence` method will get you the sequence you need. For example:

```python
import gffutils
db = create_db('file.gff', 'file.db')
for feature in db.features_of_type('CDS'):
    seq = feature.sequence('file.fa')
```

If you don't need to do anything fancy with your GFF, you might want to try `bedtools getfasta` using just the CDSs extracted (grep or awk) and the separate fasta file.

However I see in  Section 5.2 (WFN Attributes): ```Each permitted attribute MAY be used at most once in a WFN. If an attribute is not used in a WFN, it is said to be unspecified, and its value SHALL default to the logical value ANY (cf. 5.3.1)```

So it appears the current implementation conforms to that statement. I think it's a design choice if you want to conform to the reference implementation which is more strict (i.e. not omitting any attributes) or if you want to conform to the more lenient statement that omitting attributes in a wfn is permissible.

Another note about an issue to deal with when/if this issue gets more time:

On Windows, there are some edge-case bugs that cause a handshake to fail, which require dropping down to TLS 1.1 (versus TLS 1.2) or require retrying the handshake. Thus there would need to be a way to signal to the async network code to create a new socket connection and try again:

 - Dropping down to TLS 1.1 is required if the self-signed trust root used has a signature using the MD5 or MD2 hash algorithm. This is fairly rare, I believe I mostly ran into it with Chinese TLS hosts from the Alexa top 1000.
 - Retrying a connection (to get a fresh handshake) is required when using TLS 1.2 and the DHE_RSA key exchange is used and the server sends back a public key that is encoded slightly under 1024 bits (usually 127 bytes instead of 128 bytes). This is a reasonable common occurrence.

Both of these issue are currently worked around when creating a `tls.TLSSocket()` with a hostname and port. `tls.TLSSocket.wrap()` just throws an exception since it does not originate the socket.

Hi @BladeMight 

I'm going through old issues that have been forgotten. I'm sorry for dropping the ball on this one.

I understand the objective here is to type a string whenever a hotkey is pressed, and not allow the hotkey to be used again until the string has finished typing.

If that's the case, I see two solutions:
- make a thread that writes the string on a loop, but waits for a signal each iteration (could be `threading.Event`). Trigger the signal when the hotkey is pressed, and have the thread ignore signals while it's processing.
- remove the hotkey when you start typing, and re-register it after finishing.

I don't think there's anything the library could be doing differently here. Maybe a helper function to the create the signal-listening thread, but the hooks will always trigger the same function whenever the hotkey is pressed. Ignoring hotkeys is a special case.

Thanks for reporting. The issue is that the VCF header is not being copied over to the output when iterating over a VCF, so BEDTools doesn't recognize it as a proper VCF file. Using `.each()`, `.filter()`, or even `BedTool(i for i in other_bedtool)` will all cause this since they are all generators of `Interval` objects. The same error occurs on the command line if you do:

```bash
bedtools intersect -a a.vcf -b b.bed > out.vcf
bedtools intersect -a out.vcf -b b.bed
```
`bedtools intersect` has the `-header` option, so this should work as expected:
```bash
bedtools intersect -a out.vcf -b b.bed -header
```
In pybedtools it would be `out.intersect(b, header=True)`.

I'm actually not sure if there's a good solution for iterating over headers here in pybedtools. A generator of Interval files should not yield non-Interval things like header lines. Actually the same kind of problem happens on the command line if you use grep/awk without doing checks for `#` at the beginning of lines.

I think your best bet for VCF filtering will be using PyVCF/CyVCF/pysam. Then once it's filtered (with an intact header), use it with pybedtools.

Please note that the package `aiosocks` is not optimized for pip. The requested `aiohttp` version is checked [**on import**](https://github.com/nibrag/aiosocks/blob/ca6f8b9ced76924ea1d4e1c568805d6e86afef24/aiosocks/connector.py#L16) instead of [**before installing**](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp-session/blob/a1cc4efaf8314e06904c2fb030e5f598f606c9cf/setup.py#L18) the package - the latter one would request a different version in case of a version mismatch. I could file a PR to fix this upstream.
In addition a PR here would cause a huge version drop of `aiohttp` from `2.0.0 - 2.3.2`. Projects depending on `aiohttp<2.3.2` - which was just released on [2017-11-01](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/releases/tag/v2.3.2) - could not use `telepot` anymore. The problem here: we can not simply add `aiosocks` to the requirements and let pip choose a version that works with `aiohttp<2.3.2` for the _older projects_ as `aiosocks` does not specify the required version in its setup.

uuid1() is trying to prevent duplicating the guid, but its not taking into account bad system clocks or our tricks.
`uuid1()` :
```
current = gen_posix_ts
if current <= last_timestamp:
    current = last_timestamp +1
last_timestamp = current
return new_uuid_using(current)
```
So, there is really no way around this without patching up the uuid1 call (or that module in general) since it is keeping track of things itself.  I think the simplest would simply be to set uuid._last_timestamp = None on start/stop of freezegun so it doesn't play stupid games like this.

I am pretty sure that a misbehaving system clock could also land us in hot water here, at least theoretically, so this doesn't look like a real problem with freezegun itself.

The kernel not sending route updates on interface delete didn't sound right to me, so I did some digging...

For reference:
- When an IPv4 route is deleted manually, rtmsg_fib() is called here: https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/net/ipv4/fib_trie.c?h=v4.12#n1566
- When an IPv4 interface is deleted, https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/net/ipv4/fib_frontend.c?h=v4.12#n1035 calls https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/net/ipv4/fib_semantics.c?h=v4.12#n1333 which marks dependent routes with RTNH_F_DEAD, then it calls https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/net/ipv4/fib_trie.c?h=v4.12#n1871 which deletes routes marked with RTNH_F_DEAD without calling rtmsg_fib().
- This is apparently intentional behavior: http://www.spinics.net/lists/netdev/msg254186.html
- However, when an IPv6 interface is deleted, RTM_DELROUTE messages *are* sent for all dependent routes.

I don't see how you can expect asyncore/asynchat to work with a non-blocking ssl-wrapped socket when there is no handling for ssl.SSLWantReadError and ssl.SSLWantWriteError anywhere in either one.

A non-blocking ssl-wrapped socket may be indicated as readable via select/poll, but that doesn't mean there's actual user-data that can be read.  If there isn't any user-data to be read, the recv() call may raise SSLWantReadError to indicate that there is no user-data and you must wait until the underlying socket again becomes readable then retry the recv() call.  Likewise, a call to recv() may raise SSLWantWriteError to indicate that you should wait until the underlying socket is writable, then retry the recv() call.

The converse case must also be handled: calls to select/poll may indicate that  the socket is not readable even though there _is_ user data available to be read by the recv() call.

In the util.logTo{Console, File} methods there is some attempt to filter this away, like

```python
def logToConsole(level=logging.INFO):
    """
    Create a log handler that logs to the console.
    """
    logger = logging.getLogger()
    logger.setLevel(level)
    formatter = logging.Formatter(
            '%(asctime)s %(name)s %(levelname)s %(message)s')
    handler = logging.StreamHandler()
    handler.setFormatter(formatter)
    handler.addFilter(lambda record: record.name != 'root')
    logger.addHandler(handler)
```

this was by design, if an option is set all options are available on that option so that you can change it, other wise you couldn't change an option with out reseting then entire table.

For example imaging you have a table with 20 filters.  You set each filter, but at the end you decide you want red instead of green and that is the first filter.  You'd have to reset all 20 filters just to select that one, not to mention filters would then need some sort of weight and what would happen to the filters that have no options once you change the red.  they would have an option that isn't even available.

That being said I can see reasons to have and want both options.  filter-onlyAvail was meant for unfiltered selects.  Maybe we need a filter-onlyAvailFiltered or something.  Somehow I thought there was a way of doing this, I thought this was just the default behaviour.

But to answer this question yes filter-onlyAvail was designed to work this way.

I'm not sure modifying the dataset during read is the best way to handle this. After all, if the File Meta Information is missing `TransferSyntaxUID` and has compressed pixel data then by forcing `LittleEndianImplicitVR` you've just caused another error as now `pixel_array` will fail with a non-obvious cause. Plus anyone calling `Dataset.save_as()` back out without making any user changes will produce a dataset that doesn't match the original file as `file_meta` will be different.

The current code seems reasonable to me, it warns that the required element is missing from `file_meta` which suggests that it needs to be set appropriately by the user.

I was expecting that BR1, BR2, and BR3 would be using the same port for network 1 traffic and network 4 traffic and therefore it would have all kinds of work to do.  But the only traffic they should be receiving is Forwarded NPDU messages from their peers and foreign device traffic from FD2, original unicast and original broadcast messages they might get on port 47809 should be dropped or rejected.

Unlike regular BBMDs like B1 which are initialized with their directly associated IP address and port, the BIP layer of BR1, BR2, and BR3 are initialized with their "global" address and their BDTs are all full of global addresses.  The FDT will contain global addresses as well, like FD2 that sits on the Internet and registers with the public address of BR3, or the unlucky FD1 that registers with BR1 as a foreign device.

In addition to dropping original unicast and original broadcast messages, it would probably be a good idea to drop BDT and FDT maintenance traffic from outside the corporate intranet, so that would mean adding some kind of accept/reject rules.  And while we're at it, apply the same set of rules for incoming foreign device traffic to limit it to known devices.

This functionality is very close to (but subtly different from) the `BIPBBMD` class, so I'm proposing a new `BIPNAT` class.  I'll also have to build an `IPNATRouter` in the vlan module for testing . . . _no more code without being accompanied by tests, right?!_

So your reasoning is mostly correct.  It's Eliot's builtin parser and it's waiting for the next event in the level to occur sequentially, which won't happen because it doesn't exist in the data.

The good news is that eliottree has a way to filter events via the `--select` argument, which takes a [JMESPath](http://jmespath.org/) expression to apply to each logged task. So for example this should achieve what you want:
```sh
$ eliot-tree --select "level != 'debug'" normal.log
```

There's an open issue on ScatterHQ/eliot#289 about improving the documentation on this topic, if you want to follow along or suggest something that would have helped you I'm sure it would be appreciated.

Just pretend a Command is a function.

```python
from plumbum.cmd import ls
print(ls('-l', '*'))
```

Though, * will not be expanded so that will probably give you an error unless you have a file named `*`. You can expand `*` yourself:

```python
from plumbum import local
filelist = local.cwd // '*'
print(ls('-l', filelist))
```

`//` expands globs.

Thanks for the example. There are a number of issues.

The following creates strings not datetime objects. As such they get added to the Excel file as strings and not as dates:

```python
df['time'] = df['time'].apply(lambda x: datetime.fromtimestamp(x).strftime('%H:%M'))
```
It should probably be something like:

```python
df['time'] = df['time'].apply(lambda x: datetime.fromtimestamp(x))
```
See the [Working with Dates and Time](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.io/working_with_dates_and_time.html) section of the docs.

Missing date format here:
```python
worksheet.write_column('A31', df['time'])
```
Probably should be:

```python
worksheet.write_column('A31', df['time'], date_format)
```
There are a number of issues in the `x_axis` setup:

```python
chart.set_x_axis({
    'name': 'Time',
    'date_axis': True,
    'name_font': {'size': 14, 'bold': True},
    'min': "00:00",
    'max': "23:59",
    'num_format': 'hh',
    'minor_unit': 1,
    'minor_unit_type': 'hours',
    'major_unit': 5,
    'interval_unit': 5,
    'interval_tick': 2,
    'bold': 1,
    'major_unit_type': 'hours',
})
```
* The min and max should be date() or datetime() objects See [Date Category Axes](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.io/working_with_charts.html#date-category-axes).
* The `date_axis` probably doesn't work with times in Excel.
* Minor and major unit types cannot be `hours` in Excel.

However, the real problem here is that this type of chart probably isn't feasible in this way in Excel. As a minimum it probably needs a `scatter` chart rather than a `line` chart.  

I would suggest omitting the chart part of the program and working with the data in Excel to create the type of chart that you want. Then when you know the types, settings, etc., go back and add them to the XlsxWriter program.

You might have cut and paste the wrong Python 2 example.

```
>>> class Test:
...     def a(self):
...         pass
...
>>> Test.a
<unbound method Test.a>
```

As to the answer that was in SO, the Python 2 was wrong. It had:

```
>>> class X(object):
    def g():pass

>>> X.g
<unbound method X.g>
>>> class B:
    def __get__(self, *args):
        print(args)

>>> X.b = B()
>>> X.b
<__main__.B instance at 0x029C0440>
```

This will not work equivalent to the Python 3 case as the descriptor protocol only works for new style classes. Thus ``B`` should have derived from ``object``. When that is done, the result would be the same as Python 3. The ``FunctionWrapper`` in ``wrapt`` ultimately derives from ``object`` to ensure binding always works.

As to comparing Python 2 and Python 3 behaviour, using:

```
from __future__ import print_function

class X(object):
    def im(self): pass

    @staticmethod
    def sm(): pass

    @classmethod
    def cm(cls): pass

def dump(m):
    print('name', m.__name__)

    print('type', type(m))

    try:
        print('__self__', m.__self__)
    except AttributeError:
        print('__self__', '<UNDEFINED>')

    print()

dump(X.im)
dump(X().im)

dump(X.sm)
dump(X().sm)

dump(X.cm)
dump(X().cm)
```

One gets.

```
$ python2.7 bind.py
name im
type <type 'instancemethod'>
__self__ None

name im
type <type 'instancemethod'>
__self__ <__main__.X object at 0x102c500d0>

name sm
type <type 'function'>
__self__ <UNDEFINED>

name sm
type <type 'function'>
__self__ <UNDEFINED>

name cm
type <type 'instancemethod'>
__self__ <class '__main__.X'>

name cm
type <type 'instancemethod'>
__self__ <class '__main__.X'>

$ python3.4 bind.py
name im
type <class 'function'>
__self__ <UNDEFINED>

name im
type <class 'method'>
__self__ <__main__.X object at 0x10063fb00>

name sm
type <class 'function'>
__self__ <UNDEFINED>

name sm
type <class 'function'>
__self__ <UNDEFINED>

name cm
type <class 'method'>
__self__ <class '__main__.X'>

name cm
type <class 'method'>
__self__ <class '__main__.X'>
```

So Python 3 is indeed different in that it doesn't have an unbound method class.

The ``wrapt`` module, even when the method is being bound to ``None`` when the method is accessed via the class, will bind it and so return ``BoundFunctionWrapper``. This is necessary so that the desired affect is still achieved when a second binding is performed by an explicit/implicit call of ``__get__()``. There are some strange cases where this can occur which I can't remember the exact scenario for.

```
from __future__ import print_function

from wrapt import FunctionWrapper

class A(FunctionWrapper):
    A = None

    def __init__(self, f):
        super(A, self).__init__(f, self.wrapper)

    def wrapper(self, wrapped, instance, args, kwargs):
        print('wrapper', wrapped, instance, args, kwargs)
        return wrapped(*args, **kwargs)

@A
def f(*args, **kwargs):
    pass


print(type(f))
A.A = f

m = A.A
print(type(m))
print(m.__self__)

b = m.__get__(A, None)

b()
```

Technically it should have returned a ``FunctionWrapper`` rather than ``BoundFunctionWrapper``, but it was already getting quite messy so I didn't want to have a special ``UnboundFunctionWrapper`` type and instead rely on fact that for ``BoundFunctionWrapper`` the ``__self__`` attribute would be ``None``.

```
<class '__main__.A'>
<type 'BoundFunctionWrapper'>
None
wrapper <bound method ?.f of <class '__main__.A'>> <class '__main__.A'> () {}
```

```
from wrapt import synchronized
import threading
import time
from threading import Thread, RLock
import sys
import logging


log = lambda x: sys.stdout.write("%s\n" % x)


class TestObject():

    def crit(self):
        log("%s before sleep" % threading.current_thread().name)
        time.sleep(1)
        log("%s after sleep" % threading.current_thread().name)


o = TestObject()
a = Thread(target=o.crit, name="A")
b = Thread(target=o.crit, name="B")
```

> However, i've made changes to json-cfg nodes so that they auto create objects and populate them with the default values when they are missing.

This is a strategy that is very specific to your application. It mixes read and write into a relatively complex operation that isn't intuitive for the user: someone using the library (or a newcomer who reads the existing config loader code) probably doesn't expect the config data to be altered as a result of a query.

Another problem with this solution is that it works with dicts but I can easily imagine scenarios where it doesn't work in with lists (fixed length, variable length, etc...). Those situations can't be solved with a compound "query_or_set_default" operation - they require custom code (if config exists then do this, else do that).

Unfortunately for the above reasons I would be hesitant to add your solution to the core - I think it isn't simple and/or general purpose.

Supporting assignment would be relatively easy to add (at least with dicts, lists could cause problem here too) but I haven't added it due to the lack of json write support. Lists are problematic because if someone says `cfg.mylist[2].item.value = 5` then mylist has to be created, and items at index 0 and 1 have to be filled (`None` would probably be a sensible filler). Not a huge problem but isn't necessarily intuitive... Another problem is that some people might want to assign python object hierarchies like `{"x":[{"y":0}, 5], "w": {"a": []}}` to config objects and they might expect each embedded list and dict etc to be automatically wrapped into config node objects (or maybe not)... It is difficult if not impossible to solve these problems in a simple and intuitive way.

I looked into the specifics of the hashcash proof-of-work spec. On the minus side, it uses SHA1.

Format-wise, one looks like this: `"{ver}:{bits}:{date}:{resource}::{rand}:{counter}"`. Glossing over most of the details (see http://hashcash.org/docs/hashcash.html#stamp_format__version_1_) it needs a "resource" string (e.g. for email this is `user@example.com`). The `rand` valid is to prevent double-spending one token, and the `counter` is the thing you can change to try and get the whole thing to SHA1-hash to something with the right number of leading zero bits.

Perhaps we don't care what API you're calling, you just need hashcash per-connection (and thus the resource is just the "connection id"). The "resource" *could* then just be the IP address and the client doesn't have to be told an id, but it's not very easy to know what IP address you appear to the outside world (or, specifically, to the wormhole server) as.

Worth considering putting e.g. the "nameplate id" into the resource (if per-API call)?

One way I can imagine this all tying together is this:

 - upon "welcome", the server has some way to send some extra information. If it's there, it can specify whether hashcash is required (perhaps even which API methods require it?). This would include the server's notion of "connection id" for this client. It would also send the difficulty (i.e. number of bits).
 - a client can look through that and figure out if it needs to send hashcash or not (and the necessary parameters, namely the resource string and difficulty).
 - clients can send extra arguments with API requests, possibly including hashcash.
 - if the server is demanding hashcash, all requests (possibly only of one type) fail unless there's a valid hashcash token from the client.


Questions:
 - per-API-method, or just per-connection hash-cash? The former seems more flexible (e.g. maybe I only want to PoW-limit clients allocating nameplates).
 - is using hashcash *directly* a good idea, given SHA1 is burned into the spec?
 - is "announce through server 'welcome'" and "present hashcash via extra API args" a good approach?
 - do we need to count double-spends (i.e. in the database, via the rand= param as the spec intends), or is the "connection id" being in the resource string enough (because we make it unique per-connection ourselves).

@foo123 For SVM, there is the parameter probability, so you can use it to predict the probability and prediction both. But for LinearSVC in sklearn is not supported for predict_proba. But there is two ways to do this, one is use the basic SVC using the linear kernel, another is use the Probability calibration, it is a more general solution for estimator not supports predict_proba. Here is the link for it:http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/calibration.html.

For you want to get the code example, I write some basic code for you:

`
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.svm import SVC, LinearSVC
from sklearn.calibration import CalibratedClassifierCV
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import scikitplot as skplt

iris = load_iris()
x, y = iris.data, iris.target
# if you want to get the probability, set probability to be True
clf = SVC(probability=True)
clf.fit(x, y)
# get the prediction
pred = clf.predict(x)
# get the probability
prob = clf.predict_proba(x)
# you can plot the precision_recall_curve and confusion matrix
skplt.metrics.plot_precision_recall_curve(y, prob)
skplt.metrics.plot_confusion_matrix(y, pred)
plt.show()

# Here is for LinearSVC
# first way is to set the kernel of SVM to be 'linear' to replace the LinearSVC
clf = SVC(probability=True, kernel='linear')
clf.fit(x, y)
# others is same
# second way is use the CalibratedClassifierCV for more generally useful
#  for that not support predict_proba
svm = LinearSVC()
clf_cal = CalibratedClassifierCV(svm)
clf_cal.fit(x, y)
pred = clf_cal.predict(x)
prob = clf_cal.predict_proba(x)
# others is same
`

This may damage the flexibility of the config parsing. Here is my sample use-case:

I want multiple configs, including YAML, XML, INI configs to be flattened to a unified data structure, with the keys & values (and sometimes lists). The XML parser returns those `@text`, `@attrs`, `@children` keys instead of the desired values. Consider this config:

```
<config>
    <layer mode="somemode">kvm</layer>
    <env mode="static">
        <server ip="1.2.3.4"/>
        <server ip="2.3.4.5"/>
    </env>
</config>
```

The resulting structure cannot be flattened by `merge_dicts=True` option, because there are both texts, attrs & children, and the existing implementation explicitly avoid flattening in this case.

So I made my own dict-like object to flatten all keys (in `__setitem__`), so that the attrs & the children are merged as equal. The text & attrs case is, however, undecided how to handle, but probably the text part will be mapped to some predefined key. The desired result is:

```
{
  'config': {
    'layer': {
      'mode': 'somemode',
      'name': 'kvm',   # <-- text content
    },
    'env': {
      'mode': 'static',
      'pserver': [
         ...
      ],
    },
  }
}
```

How am I supposed to handle that if `to_container` is removed? Should I just post-process the results, assuming they have came from different sources (YAML vs XML), just by looking at the fields names.

E.g., if I see `@attrs`, then flatten them to the parent dict. In that case, the precedence of multiple config files can be violated (e.g., a low-priority file has attr `<env mode=static></env>` set, while the high-priority overrides it with a tag `<env><mode>static</mode></env>` — but we notice the `@attrs` and apply a low-priority value on top of the high priority value).

The self-made container could solve this problem by applying the changes on parse time of each single file.

PS: Also, `to_container` is currently broken and ignored, so it does not work. I've came here to report this, but I see that the option is going to be removed at all.

I had implement the interface at `pytablewriter 0.31.0`.
You can set alignment for each column manually by ``align_list`` attribute.
Usage is as follows:

```python
from pytablewriter import Align, MarkdownTableWriter

writer = MarkdownTableWriter()
writer.table_name = "specify alignment for each column manually"
writer.header_list = ["left", "right", "center", "auto (int)", "auto (str)", "None (auto)"]
writer.value_matrix = [
    [0, "r", "center align", 0, "a", "n"],
    [11, "right align", "c", 11, "auto", "none"],
]
writer.align_list = [Align.LEFT, Align.RIGHT, Align.CENTER, Align.AUTO, Align.AUTO, None]
writer.write_table()
```

```
# specify alignment for each column manually
|left|   right   |   center   |auto (int)|auto (str)|None (auto)|
|----|----------:|:----------:|---------:|----------|-----------|
|0   |          r|center align|         0|a         |n          |
|11  |right align|     c      |        11|auto      |none       |
```

We are having the same issue that @xuchen81 is having. From our understanding the solution proposed also should work, but from what I could read in other issues it seems to fix a MySQL error.

In our case, we had a DB in Postgres with Django 1.6. To upgrade we need to fake the initial migrations in all apps. With the original 0001_initial this works perfectly, since the schema is the same as the latest available in the south migrations.

Now, with the squashed migrations this does not work. Changes in other migrations (particularly 0003) are not executed, resulting in an inconsistent state between the models and the DB.

```

 23 | reversion | 0003_auto_20160601_1600               | 2016-07-25 20:36:42.51696+00
 24 | reversion | 0004_auto_20160611_1202               | 2016-07-25 20:36:42.535391+00
 29 | reversion | 0001_squashed_0004_auto_20160611_1202 | 2016-07-25 20:36:43.135753+00
(5 rows)

(8 rows)

dbname=# \d reversion_revision
                                     Table "public.reversion_revision"
    Column    |           Type           |                            Modifiers                            
--------------+--------------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------------
 id           | integer                  | not null default nextval('reversion_revision_id_seq'::regclass)
 date_created | timestamp with time zone | not null
```

In this case, the manager_slug field is still present, resulting in `null value in column "manager_slug" violates not-null constraint` errors.

The other issue is that the squashed migration was released simultaneously with the other migrations. So the initial migration (squashed) is actually different from the south one in 2.x releases.

A solution could be to install 1.10.2, migrate (fake-initial), then upgrade to 2.0.6 and migrate again. We are about to try that, I'll post the results.

Python has a built-in way to save and load objects to a file called `pickle`.

```python
import face_recognition
import pickle

all_face_encodings = {}

img1 = face_recognition.load_image_file("obama.jpg")
all_face_encodings["obama"] = face_recognition.face_encodings(img1)[0]

img2 = face_recognition.load_image_file("biden.jpg")
all_face_encodings["biden"] = face_recognition.face_encodings(img2)[0]

# ... etc ...

with open('dataset_faces.dat', 'wb') as f:
    pickle.dump(all_face_encodings, f)

```

That creates the file. Then later you could do this to use it:

```
import face_recognition
import pickle
import numpy as np

# Load face encodings
with open('dataset_faces.dat', 'rb') as f:
	all_face_encodings = pickle.load(f)

# Grab the list of names and the list of encodings
face_names = list(all_face_encodings.keys())
face_encodings = np.array(list(all_face_encodings.values()))

# Try comparing an unknown image
unknown_image = face_recognition.load_image_file("obama_small.jpg")
unknown_face = face_recognition.face_encodings(unknown_image)
result = face_recognition.compare_faces(face_encodings, unknown_face)

# Print the result as a list of names with True/False
names_with_result = list(zip(face_names, result))
print(names_with_result)
```

Which should print:

```
[('obama', True), ('biden', False)]
```

But yes, this just means the dlib face detector wasn't able to find a face in the image. That happens if:
1. The face is turned sideways or upside-down
2. The face is turned too sideways away from the camera instead of looking straight into the camera
3. The face is too small in the image
4. For whatever other reason, the face detector just failed

Depending on why the face wasn't detected, you can try different things. For example if you have sideways or upside-down images, make sure to rotate them back so they are right ways up before running face recognition. This situation happens a lot with cellphone images where the image itself is captured sideways.

Ok, I think I figured out what's going on. I recently separated suffixes into 2 groups, ones that are acronyms and could be separated by periods, and ones that are not. So I guess this means updating the `suffixes` set now has no effect. You'll need need to add your custom suffixes to `CONSTANTS.suffix_acronyms` in this case, or `CONSTANTS.suffix_not_acronyms`.

```
>>> from nameparser import HumanName
>>> from nameparser.config import CONSTANTS
>>> CONSTANTS.suffix_acronyms.add('msc')
>>> HumanName("Dr. Margit Popp MSc")
<HumanName : [
   title: 'Dr.' 
   first: 'Margit' 
   middle: '' 
   last: 'Popp' 
   suffix: 'MSc'
   nickname: ''
]>
```

> Fork would duplicate nearly everything, though not immediately (thanks to copy-on-write). Is that bad?

Fork clones just 1 process. The threads are not cloned (but they will have broken references to them around, like the `Thread` objects).

This happens because threads in Linux are just special processes, and fork doesn't clone multiple processes.

Hi @capiscuas 

``refresh()`` method on the resource object is making a new GET request to get all the possible information about a resource from the Redmine. It can't have any kind of cache since to have this cache Python-Redmine would still need to make a request at some point. If we take a user resource as an example it will need to make one request per user since login attribute isn't available if you request all user at once.

If you already have a dict in memory with all the user attributes, it is definitely better to take the needed data from it than using a ``refresh()`` method.

As far as I'm aware the output will be stored in either the static root or the specified sass root. I should've been more specific and that might be because this was very late (or early), but my idea was to output them in a path relative to the input SCSS-file.

```
root/
    app/
        scss/
            app.scss
        static/
            css/
                app.css
    app2/
        scss/
            app2.scss
        static/
            css/
                app2.css
```

Instead of:
```
root/
    app/
        scss/
            app.scss
    app2/
        scss/
            app2.scss
    static/
        css/
            app.css
            app2.css
```

Both `app/static`, `app2/static`, `app/scss`, and `app2/scss` would then be in `STATICFILES_DIRS`. Perhaps make `SASS_PROCESSOR_ROOT` possible to be a path relative to the source SCSS file?

```python
    HANDLER_LEVEL = application['log_level']

    # flask attributes: accessible across application
    app.config.update(
        HOST=general['host'],
        CACHE_HOST=cache['host'],
        CACHE_PORT=cache['port'],
        CACHE_DB=cache['db'],
        ROOT=ROOT,
        SQL_HOST=sql['host'],
        SQL_LOG_PATH=sql['log_path'],
        SQL_DB=sql['name'],
        SQL_USERNAME=sql['username'],
        SQL_PASSWORD=sql['password'],
        NOSQL_HOST=nosql['hostname'] + ':' + str(nosql['net']['port']),
        NOSQL_DB=nosql['name'],
        NOSQL_USERNAME=nosql['username'],
        NOSQL_PASSWORD=nosql['password'],
        LOG_LEVEL=HANDLER_LEVEL,
        FLASK_LOG_PATH=webserver['flask']['log_path'],
        ERROR_LOG_PATH=application['error_log_path'],
        WARNING_LOG_PATH=application['warning_log_path'],
        INFO_LOG_PATH=application['info_log_path'],
        DEBUG_LOG_PATH=application['debug_log_path'],
        MODEL_TYPE=application['model_type'],
        MAXCOL_ANON=application['dataset']['anonymous']['max_collection'],
        MAXDOC_ANON=application['dataset']['anonymous']['max_document'],
        MAXCOL_AUTH=application['dataset']['authenticated']['max_collection'],
        MAXDOC_AUTH=application['dataset']['authenticated']['max_document'],
        SALT_LENGTH=crypto['salt_length'],
        SCRYPT_N=crypto['scrypt_n'],
        SCRYPT_R=crypto['scrypt_r'],
        SCRYPT_P=crypto['scrypt_p'],
        PASSWORD_MIN_C=validate_password['password_min_c'],
        PASSWORD_MAX_C=validate_password['password_max_c'],
        USER_ID=0
    )

    # log format
    formatter = logging.Formatter(
        "[%(asctime)s] {%(pathname)s:%(lineno)d} "
        "%(levelname)s - %(message)s"
    )

    # initialize the log handler
    handler = RotatingFileHandler(
        LOG_PATH,
        maxBytes=10000000,
        backupCount=5
    )

    # log handler level
    handler.setLevel(HANDLER_LEVEL)
    handler.setFormatter(formatter)
    app.logger.addHandler(handler)

    # app logger level
    log = logging.getLogger('werkzeug')
    log.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
    log.addHandler(handler)

    # return
    app.config['PROPAGATE_EXCEPTIONS'] = False
    return app
```

So `force_bytes` and `force_bytestring` really only do that, that is, get you into a situation where given instance is converted to a string. Neither of them is designed to make cache keys.

Now that I look at the state of django utils I don't think any already available function will give proper results, since `str` is not proper solution for cache keys. You can use string as cache keys, but not every result of `str(object)` makes a good key - that's why we have `hash`.

Since the `__hash__` is [documented](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/ref/models/instances/#hash) I think we can safely add the basic handling of this case, where, if we have Django model instances as an element of args/kwargs, we call `hash` on it.

It has a few advantages:

 * for vanilla Django objects it will work properly
 * if somebody overrode `__str__` but not `__hash__` it will work properly
 * if somebody overrode `__hash__` - it will work properly

The proper solution is to use Django's built-in many to many relation. It's well supported on all databases, it's native to Django, and it gives you much more flexibility and robustness than this project ever could or will.

This project should only be used if you are absolutely positively sure you will never need to query on any of the fields ever, or if you need to add some application-layer logic to a legacy database column.

Yes, that is [as designed](https://github.com/wimglenn/djangorestframework-queryfields/blob/3b3c2e78ae25906c3e4eb8ee1495bf8c3d105258/tests/test_userfields_with_plain_serializer.py#L133-L142).  

Since DRF uses a serializer on POST requests to deserialize and validate (when creating an instance), and it also uses a serializer to return a representation of the created instance, there would be ambiguity here on which serializer the fields should apply to.  Any effects for POST requests are explicitly disabled and `fields=` is ignored.  

If you have heavy fields you don't want serialized it might be better to exclude those all the time in the response to POST.  You can do that in DRF directly, we don't need queryfields for that.  But if the fields returned in a newly created instance need to be customized by the client on a per-request basis, then maybe I can extend queryfields to support this behaviour - please clarify the use case required?

**Problem:** It is hard to train large network. It's simpler to start with smaller layer size and incrementally increase the size along the training. In such way it will also reduce the chance of overfitting.

This design is certainly unconventional and takes me a while to come up with it, in a sense that it tries to achieve something that would otherwise impossible to achieve. Introducing TVM support to MXNet NDArray without a direct link dependency so a user can try it out anytime by using the default binary distro. 

The complication and benefit bough by MXNet's async execution makes the callback wrapping style case a bit tricky. If the function call had been simple CUDA calls things would be simpler. But thankfully the logic is clear enough.

With this we can do things like write customized update rule in TVM while still JIT compile to get the best speedups

@gigasquid are you using an NVIDIA GPU on a Mac? If so, you might want to consider downgrading your NVIDIA Web Drivers. >Use the copy method to copy between objects in the same location and storage class. The implementation of loop-less transport is here: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiozmq/blob/master/aiozmq/core.py#L667

While it's based on `Transport` protocol the code may be transplanted into [`zmq.asyncio.Socket`](https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/blob/master/zmq/asyncio/__init__.py#L273-L298) easily.

The key point is not adding writer but getting notification file descriptor by `fd = zmq_sock.getsockopt(zmq.FD)` and subscribing for events from it.

The tricky part of implementation is: after every read/write operation the file descriptor should be requested again for upcoming events and if there are events to process `.read_ready()` should be scheduled again on the next loop iteration.

From my understanding it's because `zmq.FD` works on edge-triggered level, not level triggered one.
@tailhook had consulted me about the patch, he knows better how zeromq works in this mode.

i am a computer graphics (CG) researcher and an entrepreneur in that field, so please be aware that my comments are biased by my background.

in CG it is extremely important to be able to handle both:
- point clouds: an unordered set of points;
- meshes; an ordered set of points linked by faces (e.g., with connectivity/topology information);

if the question is: "should assimp import files with vertex-only data and no topology?" then my answer is "definitely yes". i had to bypass assimp in those situations because it failed or gave me an arbitrary meshing.

if the question is: "should assimp generate meshes from a point cloud input?" then my answer is "not now and never ever": this is a tough and open task, researchers on this field are publishing papers every year. assimp should import point cloud and meshes to a common format, by a deterministic processing, and not performing an arbitrary (and painful) 3D reconstruction.

How are you using the presigned url?
For what it's worth you only need to provide the customer algorithm on the presigned url:
```
url = s3client.generate_presigned_url('get_object', Params={
        "Bucket": aws_data["bucket"],
        "Key": upload_filename,
        "SSECustomerAlgorithm": "AES256"})
```

Then when you use the url you need to provide the appropriate headers:
```
curl --header 'x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-algorithm:AES256' --header 'x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-key:<keyhere>' --header 'x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-key-md5:<md5hash>' <presignedurl>
```

I had this same problem using the CLI. If the lambda function has a timeout higher than 60 seconds and it runs longer than this time then this will cause a timeout and repeated invocations. 

I opened an issue aws/aws-cli#2657 in the aws-cli repo about this. When using the CLI it is possible to solve it by passing "--cli-read-timeout" as an argument. There are a couple of options to automatically get the lambda timeout instead of adding an hardcoded number by adding an alias to the CLI.

I see that in Boto3 the timeout can be changed in the config so it should be possible to write a helper that gets the lambda timeout value and changes the configuration before invoking the function.

Fixed by #662.

You cannot get this kind of error due to `Index`. The file is closed as soon as the contents are read, and IIRC it's closed even before we start parsing the contents.

What does stay open (and it's what the AppVeyor logs show) are the packfile indices. These are kept open since we memory-map them since they're read-only and are potentially large. In order to close those handles, you need to make sure to free the underlying `git_repository`, which means making sure any `Repository` instances get garbage-collected.

You will then have to work around the object db being made out of read-only files, as Windows won't let you delete them normally, and it's usually a pain to deal with in test suites.

But fixing this in the test suite isn't going to fix anything else using pygit2. Whatever tool you're using must make sure to release its `Repository` instances on Windows if you want to delete repositories while the program is still running.

The general idea for the priority of the configuration params is borrowed from the way command-line tools configure themselves; usually there is a list of configuration sources (rough description follows):

```
1. source-code "default" kwds
2. source-code kwds
3. system conf-file
4. user conf-file
5. env-vars
6. cmd-line args
```

So based on the list above, `5. env-vars` override `2. source-code kwds` which is at the bottom.

BUT *gitpython* is a not a Cmd-line tool but a Library, and function arguments can be though of as the equivalent of the `6. cmd-line args`, so they must have the top-most priority, and what @ethereon asks makes sense.  

Taking a strict stance on that would mean that env-vars (such as `GIT_DIR`) are not expanded by the the library-code, and they should be left for the end-user to decide.  Unfortunately there are places where this decision (for the the var-expansion and override) happens internally, in code-paths that are not under the direct control of the end-user, so this "strict stance" is too idealistic to be functioning.

 Any ideas on this?

This isn't that simple after all.

The simple part was the new KW's for storing the session and restoring it. That is now implemented, as expected, without changing any of the code for the existing KW's.

**But things get a bit complicated, because BrowserCache might get duplicate entries:**
Assume 2 TC's: "open and store session" and "restore session"
Executing them like this is fine:
- `pybot -t "open and store session" suite.robot`
- `pybot -t "restore session" suite.robot`

because the BrowserCache is not persistent between executions.

But this will cause problems:
- `pybot -t "open and store session" -t "restore session" suite.robot`

because the BrowserCache will after two sessions pointing to the same Browser --> things like "Close All Browsers" will fail because when trying to close the 2nd session the browser is already closed.

I changed the BrowserCache so that on `register()` it checks the session ID and if that session ID already exists the old cache entry is marked obsolete (i.e. added to the `self._closed` list)

**Things get even more complicated with the WebDriver Service process management:**
In the standard use case: `Create Webdriver` + `Close Browser` following things happen:
1) `Create Webdriver`: 
1.1) starts the WebDriver Service (e.g. chromedriver.exe) and stores a reference to it
1.2) opens a selenium session and stores it inside selenium (e.g. capabilities, session ID, URL) and s2l (i.e. BrowserCache)
2) `Close Browser`
2.1) closes the browser and deletes session (inside selenium and s2l)
2.2) stops the WebDriver service

The WebDriver service part is tricky, because 
- each browser has it's own Service management implementation (inside selenium) e.g. some could be just terminated with a kill-signal and some with a HTTP request e.g. "POST /STOP"
- to use selenium's service interface one would need to re-instantiate the "old" service class instance (e.g. with serialization) and connect it to already running process (AFAIK this is not possible)

To solve this I implemented a generic "brute-force" approach where 
- the PID of the service process is stored in the `Save Webdriver` KW and
- the `Restore Webdriver` saves this PID in the session
- When such a session is closed (e.g. with `Close Browser`) it simply tries to kill (with signal.SIGTERM) a process with that PID. If the kill fails a warning log is written.

This is a bit flaky, because I'm not so sure:
- how the os.kill() behaves on different OS's - does it kill the process reliably.
- how the different WebDriver processes behave: e.g. after termination with SIGTERM: is the socket closed in a controlled way, if not the port might stay in TIME_WAIT state and starting a new WebDriver process might fail because the port is still reserved.

_So, what shall we do with this feature?_
IMHO this is very valuable functionality and I really would like to see it as part of the s2l.
On the other hand, it looks like that it'll be very hard to have a 100% reliable implementation that works with all browsers and on all OS's.
My proposal would be to:
- have the new KW's and document the limitations in the KW documentation (e.g. something like "Tested with FF & GC on Windows 10 and Linux, other browser+OS combinations might work.")
or
- make the new KW's private (and document the limitations) + gather more experiences with the new KW's, and eventually make the KW's public later on.

The code can be found in https://github.com/yahman72/Selenium2Library/tree/reusable_session

@mteiste thanks for your input, I didn't make my decision based on test failures but rather based on specific concerns about the implementation around removal of temporary file objects which are owned by forked process groups on windows. I closed this PR because you actually have no way of knowing if the file descriptor is actually in use based simply on your own context manager, because forked processes (e.g. pip) are explicitly passed those resources.

More exhaustively, processes forked in `pip_install` are not all returned or completed there, which is one of many reasons why simply calling `os.unlink` is not a safe operation on windows. This code is not the sole owner of the file, so when the `unlink` inevitably fails due to another process reading it (that is sufficient to cause `unlink` to fail on windows), there is no wait or retry here.

>  if the unlink() fails due to errors like file handle leakage issues in windows subprocess handling, I cannot comment, but I would imagine we've lost at that time anyways :)

Windows is a first class citizen, so this is unfortunately not going to work as an approach. I'm sorry about this pull request, but I didn't close it out of spite or ignorance, there is legitimately a lot of nuance underlying this that I just finished sorting out with our tests. I am far from an expert but I do know that the naive approach doesn't work in this case

This is an old debate and there are no I’ve solution. Libraires need to define their dependencies in their install_require section of setup.py. Application may skip this and store their dependencies in requirements.txt/Pipfile. Pinning version is always tricky, so for app you may want to use pip-compile or pipenv.

In order to keep a single source of truth for libraries, in a requirement.txt/Pipfile (no lock file, because you don’t want to pin dep for libs), you need an extra layer of abstraction. I choose PBR (inject requirements.txt in install_require) and pipenv-to-requirement (generate requirement.txt from Pipfile) to still maintain a Pipfile in a library. But that’s a personal choice and most lib developers simply maintain a setup.py without requirements/Pipfile. Especially since they cannot pin the version because you want your lib to work on many configuration.

On the new version of LZ this is working, but it has a problem:  when the label and tooltip both extend from a point in the same direction, then when you hover the label, the tooltip pops up, meaning that now you're on the tooltip, not the label, and so the tooltip goes away, and you get a nasty cycle (limited to one show/hide per pixel the mouse moves).

(Old-PheWAS didn't have this problem because the tooltips were always above the points, and labels always beside.)

We need to keep the current behavior that hovering a tooltip has no effect.

*Option 1*: when you hover a label, the tooltip avoids the label.  But if a point is near the bottom-left corner, and the label goes straight right, then what?  We can't move the label.  Where's the tooltip supposed to go?  Tooltips don't know how to go above/below points, and they certainly don't know how to extend further away from a point.  But if they could go up/down, and lengthen the connecting triangle, and would avoid the label when they popped up, that would solve this problem, right?

*Option 2*: When the tooltip pops up, you're still hovering it, even though you can't see it.  ie, tooltips are invisible from the mouse's perspective.  Look into <http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1401658/html-overlay-which-allows-clicks-to-fall-through-to-elements-behind-it>.

> Do we really need such complex "infastructure" for this simple tutorial?

There's one place that we do: form handling.

The naive approach to handling input in a web app is actively dangerous.  It can easily allow any attacker that can get an authenticated user to click on _any_ website to perform authenticated operations on yours.  [See here for more detail on this specific attack.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-site_request_forgery)  This why the branch is called "form hacks".

We don't _need_ SQL for a tutorial - but then, your proposed example already has SQL in it, it's just injection-prone plain-text SQL rather than properly expression-object data access as with SQLAlchemy or similar.  And everyone is going to want to have some kind of backing database anyway.

I do think the tutorial should start out as light as possible, and introduce concepts gradually, but like it or not Klein _is_ a web framework, and I think this example should be complete enough to prevent people from shooting themselves in the foot :).

Hi All,  

At bobgrigoryan's suggestion I'll repost what I've found regarding this issue here.  I'll also point out that the 'bug' seems to be in the java code (project.clj) that bob mentions above.  As a python hack I fixed the problem with an ugly change to run.py.  I just wanted to get a topology running to learn.  IMHO the problem seems to be that 'resources' is inserted twice into the path that contains the tmp files of spouts and bolts:

From the stream parse forum:
------------------------------------
I'm interested in learning the streamparse library and brought up an install on my mac laptop via:

mkvirtualenv storm-1.0.3
working storm-1.0.3

brew storm install
pip streamparse install

... etc.

created the wordcount topology

cd word count
sparse run

which would eventually fail with an error message (included below) that essentially said "I can't find the spouts or the bolts to create the toplogy"

so I poked around and after a bit of trial and error it seems that (on my machine anyway) that the directory from which run.py was executing was one level too high and that the spouts and bolts were buried another level deeper in the temporary tree...

In other words, run.py was executing with os.getcwd() equal to 

/private/var/folders/_1/p0xw6sw92kn2g_mnh9ggl160000107/T/54658c95-597d-4fd5-a67d-9209e8e0ac31/supervisor/stormdist/wordcount-1-1490153387/resources/

but in order to see the bolts and spouts, the directory needed to be:

/private/var/folders/_1/p0xw6sw92kn2g_mnh9ggl160000107/T/54658c95-597d-4fd5-a67d-9209e8e0ac31/supervisor/stormdist/wordcount-1-1490153387/resources/resources/

I don't know if this error is reproducible and i haven't looked into why 'resources' is in the path twice, however, i fixed it temporarily with a change to run.py:

diff run.py run.old.py

31c31

<     sys.path.extend((os.getcwd(), os.getcwd()+'/resources'))

---

>     sys.path.append(os.getcwd())



which is ugly but at least I can now run topologies locally.

Posting here in case anyone else is running a local topology on a mac (probably a small set of folks)

import os

from collections import Counter



from streamparse import Bolt





class WordCountBolt(Bolt):

    outputs = ['word', 'count']



    def initialize(self, conf, ctx):

        self.counter = Counter()

        self.pid = os.getpid()

        self.total = 0



    def _increment(self, word, inc_by):

        self.counter[word] += inc_by

        self.total += inc_by



    def process(self, tup):

        word = tup.values[0]

        self._increment(word, 10 if word == "dog" else 1)

        if self.total % 1000 == 0:

            self.logger.info("counted [{:,}] words [pid={}]".format(self.total,

                                                                    self.pid))

        self.emit([word, self.counter[word]])

I like this idea, but it would require separate virtualenvs per component in the topology.  We're going to end up having to do that anyway to avoid race conditions when we switch to using the topology hook to address #99, but it's worth noting.

I'm not sure if you're advocating actually using the object hook facilities in simplejson/json.  If so, the performance consequences of this are monstrous.  Last I looked, it's more than two orders of magnitude slower when you use it.  Don't quote me on that, but I can look it up or run some tests if need be.

I know we *can* add that and say "use at your own risk," but the performance implications are so large that it feels disingenuous to even use it.  It's more like "we implemented it, but never use it if you care about throughput. Ever.".

If you're saying we implement something *like* that facility where we give the raw input and people can handle their own encoding/decoding, that's not a bad idea at all.

This is important for me as well, we're using Streamparse to deploy Machine Learning models. Compiling numerical and machine learning libraries during deployment is very painful and takes a lot of time :)

Hi @sazlin, thanks for the report!

We've added some magic to make our list objects behave more like lists (despite the fact that they're really objects that _contain_ a list). I think this issue is caused by the [`__len__`](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/blob/f948b8b95b6df5b57c7444a05d6c83c8c5e6a0ac/stripe/api_resources/list_object.py#L55-L56) method. If I comment it out, empty lists are serialized correctly.

When the method is present, non-empty list objects are still serialized correctly even if the method doesn't return the number of attributes of the list object but rather the length of the `data` attribute, so I suspect that somewhere in the JSON serialization code, there is a `if len(obj) == 0` check.

I'll investigate a bit more and update with my findings here.

```
*Subject:* FW: Open-source implementation of sub-pixel registration
*Date:* Wed 07 Jan 2015 11:25:47 AM PST

Attached are some comments on the Python implementation of our
registration algorithm by my student, Alex. I agree that it should allow
for either images or Fourier transforms to be input, and that images are
allowed to be complex-valued (important for coherent imaging such as for
medical ultrasound, synthetic-aperture radar, etc.), and that "phase
correlation" is not descriptive enough -- it is image registration by
cross-correlation.

I wish you success in getting this out to the Python community. Our group
(well, most of it) is transitioning from Matlab to Python.

Best regards,

James R. Fienup

On 1/7/15 11:07 AM, "Iacchetta, Alexander" wrote:

>Dr. Fienup,
>
>This scikit-image software mimics Manuel's Matlab code pretty well.  I
>mostly have suggestions that affect usage, but I also have a conceptual
>objection to the use of a filter function.  I tried to make my remarks as
>detailed as possible
>
>With regard to the filters, the intended usage is unclear and will likely
>decrease accuracy filtering the source image will change the magnitudes
>in the frequency domain.  Two identical images shifted by some amount
>will differ only in phase in the frequency domain, so a convolution with
>a filter function in the frequency domain on only one of the images could
>reduce accuracy.  Perhaps there is another reason why the filter function
>is included, but I suggest that all aspects of the filter function should
>be removed.  Another reason to remove the filter functions all together
>is that there is not enough control over the filters to have a broad use.
>
>If the filter functions are not removed, they will need a very minor
>modification.  The filters that are applied should be generated with
>"sym=False" to ensure that the DC pixel of the filter matches the DC
>pixel of the image (this has a very minor affect but shouldn't be
>ignored, see below).  The documentation should specify that filters are
>only used when image domain arrays are provided.
> --> line 101 of phase_correlate.py should be changed to "window =
>filter_function(axis_size, sym=False).reshape(filter_shape)" from "window
>= filter_function(axis_size).reshape(filter_shape)"
>
>Currently, the software makes assumptions about the domain of the images
>based on being real or complex arrays.  Whether or not the input arrays
>are in the image or frequency plane should be set by a flag, not with
>real/complex assumptions, so that complex images can also be registered.
>The error message that occurs when one input is real and one is complex
>should also be removed to allow for registering complex images (lines
>162-165).
>
>The name of the function could lead to some confusion, and should be more
>indicative of image registration.
>
>A final consideration is to use a Fourier filter to perform the shift
>operation if the example script plot_phase_correlate.py instead of spline
>interpolation.  For example:
>        def fourier_shift(image, shift):
>            rows =
>numpy.fft.ifftshift(np.arange(-numpy.floor(image.shape[0]/2),
>numpy.ceil(image.shape[0]/2)))
>            cols =
>numpy.fft.ifftshift(np.arange(-numpy.floor(image.shape[1]/2),
>numpy.ceil(image.shape[1]/2)))
>            cols, rows = numpy.meshgrid(cols, rows)
>            out = numpy.fft.ifft2(numpy.fft.fft2(image)*
>numpy.exp(1j*2*pi*(shift[0]*rows/image.shape[0]+shift[1]*cols/image.shape[
>1])))
>            return out
>
>Let me know if there are any questions.
>
>Alex
```

I have benchmarked cluster speed using our typical load of reads/writes, with object sizes on average are around 4kb.  Only btrfs and ext4 managed to get up to tens of thousands of operations a second, whereas xfs only managed to peak at 1000 initially (and went downhill from there).

Using btrfs in a modest sized production environment has proved that btrfs weighs more negatively on continuous maintenance over the positive throughput and speed.

Meanwhile, in our pre-Ceph storage system, we have been using ext4 for years with no problems, so I'd like to continue using it for the moment while still using ceph-deploy to manage the cluster.

One more thing. I'd like to discuss __aggregation__ . 
There's a parameter `aggregationWindow`(=5), 
- in Python I think this reduces data for use in `updateAnomalies()` from, say, `25`(/5) => 4. 
- I've mistakenly implemented this as `running average`(of size 5), so I'd get 25 values (smoothed by 5). 
  - computational complexity aside, this could actually be a better approach: the statistics is ~same, you get more detailed data, but still get some rounding (smoothing). 

- Overall, I'd like to propose to __remove__ this functionality from the C++ (maybe from Py too?), as it
  - adds hidden complexity
  - can be easily implemented by the client( `auto a5 = anomalyRaw(with window=5).compute(); if(iter++%5==0) auto score = likelihood.compute(a5);` )
   - is conceptually not optimal (even wrong). If you need to do aggregation, do it on the lower levels (input layer), as it also affects TM (which is used to Likelihoods). 

From the comments I can guess the example for which this was developed is 
> HotGym consumption: 5min data, cycle 1week, we want to predict&detect hourly changes

So there was aggregation used to condense {12x5min} => 1hr. 

TL;DR: aggregation should be done (and is supported) on lower levels (input), not in Likelihood. Remove it from there.

`update` is called 
~12M times; Time: 77s, Own-Time: 25s

~12M times; Time: 52s, Own-Time: 32s
`<built-in method now>` (`Datetime.now()`):
~12M times; Time: 20s, Own-Time: 20s

So the `25s + 32s + 20s = 77s` are the runtime of the update method (77s are ~18% of complete runtime from cities_light in my case) and is generated only by calculations in these 3 methods.

```python
    p = P(text=u'text')
    df = odf.draw.Frame( zindex=0, anchortype='as-char')
    p.addElement(df)

    math = Math()
    annot = Element(qname = (MATHNS,u'annotation'))

    math.addElement(annot)
    do = odf.draw.Object()
    do.addElement(math)
    df.addElement(do)

    outputfile = u'result'
    doc.save(outputfile, True)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

Let's think through some of the behavior specifics.

- What happens if the file already has a main()? What if it's already using Fire or some other CLI library? Probably we don't want `__name__ == '__main__'` to be True when we run code.py, but inevitably if code.py has top-level code then it will be executed.
- Python supports 'python -m' for running a module. Do we want to support 'fire -m' as well?
- With regular Fire CLIs, we need to separate flags from the fire command with an isolated '--'. Here we could potentially allow the fire flags to preceed the filename. e.g. `fire -i code.py` or `fire -h code.py command`. Is this something we'd want?
- I have a design doc coming for updating tab completion; we'll have to think about how this would play alongside tab completion.

Virtuoso [seems to allow](https://github.com/openlink/virtuoso-opensource/issues/126) <NodeID://b0005> for blank nodes, and Jena's [ARQ query engine](https://jena.apache.org/documentation/query/extension.html#blank-node-labels) allows <_:0005> to refer to blank nodes by ID. So there are at least two alternative syntaxes to support !

So I think turning bNodeAsURI into a function is a great idea, so the SPARQLStore class can work with these various different SPARQL engines.

In terms of results, they are returned in XML form, and blank nodes appear as <ns:bnode> elements. The CastToTerm function already handles those and converts them to rdflib.BNode() instances.

The patch I submitted should make SPARQLUpdateStore work the same as SPARQLStore; I haven't actually tested that it does, though.

I'm happy to rework this to take a function instead of a boolean, I wonder if you can advise me on how the API should look, though? I thought about renaming 'bNodeAsURI' to 'bnode_as_uri' already, but I thought that it would cause an API break for anyone who is currently calling plugins.sparqlstore.SPARQLStore(..., bNodeAsURI=False, ...). Renaming it bNodeToURI would cause the same thing. So maybe it's best to keep the name the same, and allow it to be either False, or a function that returns the blank node as a string ?

Actually, whiten technically works, but only because it adds so much noise that the asymmetry in the correlations are small relative to the dc term.  The symmetrize tests still fail when the wcs and psf are not symmetric.

I think I understand why strategy 1 doesn't work.  This is where the noise model is kept track of automatically.  The estimate of the noise correlation function gets too small a maxk from the convolution by the PSF.  I don't have an easy fix to this, but at least I think I understand why it fails.

For strategy 2, I compute the correlation function from a noise field directly and then use that to symmetrize.  I don't yet understand why this one fails.  That might be my next task on this issue.  I think we should be able to make that one work.  

However, I think strategy 4 will still be preferred in practice, since it actually adds less noise than either strategy 1 or 2.  And it's faster than strategy 2.  So unless we can get strategy 1 working (which is the fastest method), there doesn't seem to be any reason to switch.

Considering that this would probably be an API change (although we could certainly keep the old name as well for backwards compatibility), it might be a good time to try to decide on this and get it into v2.0.

Suggestions:
- `galsim.getCOSMOSNoise(file, rng, cosmos_scale,...)` -> `galsim.CorrelatedNoise.from_file(file, rng, scale,...)`
- `galsim.COSMOSCatalog(file, sample,...)` keeps current name, but is a thin wrapper around a `galsim.GalaxySample` or `galsim.TrainingSample` which would be the generic back end that could include AEGIS as well.
- `COSMOS` noise type in config keeps current name, but builder would be a subclass of `CorrelatedNoiseBuilder`, which would handle arbitrary input files.
- `cosmos_catalog` -> `galaxy_sample` or `training_sample` to match above name change.
- `COSMOSGalaxy` -> `SampleGalaxy` or `TrainingGalaxy`
- `scene.py` could also maybe change to something that better matches the name we come up with for the training sample catalog class.

`__eq__` should return whether two objects are precisely equal, not just close.  It's probably not "used for" much -- we define it for all GalSim classes to override the default python implementation, which is equivalent to `is`.

The round trip through the fits header doesn't need to be perfect.  It should be possible to make it accurate to pretty good accuracy though, possibly by writing extra (proprietary) header keys.  I've been starting them with `GS_` when I do this.  But even then, we're always limited by the finite number of digits stored in fits header values, so there's only so much we can do.

Once you get most of the functionality set, I can take a look and see if I want to tighten that up.  But don't worry about it too much now if you've already got it reasonably close for a round trip.
