@rmjarvis - In the course of implementing your suggestion about the random rotations, I realized a pretty stunning bug in the demo: we have made ```final_image``` appropriately with the WCS, but then we draw the galaxy image into a postage stamp for which we haven't even specified a pixel scale (OY!), using
```
stamp = galsim.Image(stamp_size, stamp_size)
gal_list[i_gal].drawImage(filter_, image=stamp)
```
Then we add that into ```final_image```.  So this makes no sense whatsoever.  I think we have two options:

(1) If we want to retain the original advantage of only having to draw the galaxy once, then we need to set ```stamp``` to have the average WFIRST pixel scale, and comment that this is an approximation and really one needs to draw into ```final_image``` which has the full WCS if you want to get this right.

(2) Scrap the idea of having many copies of the galaxies in the image, make a sparsely-populated image with a single copy of each galaxy, and draw the galaxies appropriately into ```final_image```.

@arunkannawadi , @rmjarvis , any preferences?  I'm inclined to go with (1), and will shortly push a version that does that, but I could be persuaded to switch to (2) (and it would be easy).

Looking on your log files I can imagine that your etcd cluster is not behaving nicely, probably because there is not enough IO capacity on the filesystem where etcd writes its wals and snapshots.
How I come to such conclusion? Log lines containing only keys Patroni trying to create or update (/initialize, /members/node2) and there is no exception for recursive read request, which fetches entire cluster.  Reads are not failing because etcd serves them from memory, but for writes it needs to write data into a wal file.

An Iteration entity has attributes of Project, StartDate and EndDate.  You can use a pyral Rally instance get method targeting Iteration like so:
...
rally = Rally(*your options including credentials, workspace, project, etc.)
result = rally.get('Iteration')
You could then iterate through the result, checking to see that your target date >= StartDate and target date <= EndDate (assuming your target date is today's date).  
Alternatively, you could construct query conditions that expressed the same logic and provide those to the get call so that the selection logic executes on the WSAPI receiving end.

Yes, and this is what we do with the specific `to_json()` or a more generic `conv.jsonify_value()` (which does not fit your need).

The general approach which is used through all the application code distinguishes Python data (objects, lists, dicts and data types), JSON-like data (lists, dicts and JSON compatible types, but still Python), and JSON-serialized string. `respond_json` is just the end of the chain which is not responsible for transforming Python data into JSON string, but just JSON-like data into JSON string.

This is just the way it has been design.

I was also having this problem when using an S3 compatible system that has a certificate signed by a custom CA. I had already configured my `centos:7.5.1804` to trust that CA -- both `curl` and `java` connect fine to the https endpoint. But apparently centos/rhel `update-ca-trust` does not update the CAs trusted by python 3 / requests. 

python requires the below environment variable to be defined:

```
# test -z "${EXTRA_CA}" || { echo "${EXTRA_CA}" > /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/ca.cer && update-ca-trust || exit $? ; }
# export REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE=/etc/pki/ca-trust/extracted/openssl/ca-bundle.trust.crt
# s4cmd --endpoint-url https://s3compatible.example.test mb s3://mybucket
```

To add on @neubig 's comments:

1) Yes, you do need to create the graph each time, but this construction is very fast.
2) The Python bindings do support "static" graphs to some extent: you can construct a graph and then change just the input nodes. (I am not sure this holds for the inputTensor yet).
3) But you really shouldn't care much about doing (2), as creating the graph is really as fast once you start doing any "real" computation. Adapt your thinking to how DyNet works.
4) In particular, numpy ndarrays and DyNet internal matrices don't share the same memory, so there is a memory copy whenever you create a DyNet inputNode from a numpy array OR when you "set" the numpy array of an already existing node. And the memory copy is by far the dominating factor speed-wise in these operations.

> At the moment, one can use a batch or a transaction as a context manager an *not* pass it everywhere, or call methods through it.  E.g.:

> ```python
from gcloud import datastore
with datastore.Transaction():
    keys = [entity.key for entity in some_list]
    datastore.delete(keys)
```

> This works because the `_require_connection` code is willing to look up the current batch / transaction from a thread-local stack, falling back to use the global default.  Calling API functions through the notional `Client` instance will defeat that stacking.  Note that a object methods will be also lose that stacking.

I'm proposing that we make people be a bit more explicit for batches and transactions:

```python
from gcloud import datastore
client = datastore.Client() #  This guesses the credentials and project ID magically
# or
client = datastore.Client(project='project-id', key_path='/path/to/key.json')

keys = [ .... ]

with client.Transaction() as t:
  t.delete(keys)

# or

with client.Transaction():
  client.delete(keys)
```

Wouldn't this make the "require connection" stuff just go away all together? We never guess, or look to the global scope, or anything like that...

Note (as a follow-up from issue #132):
```
>>> import collections
>>> X = collections.namedtuple('Y', ['a','b'])
>>> Xi = X(0,1)
>>> import dill
>>> dill.detect.errors(Xi)
PicklingError("Can't pickle <class '__main__.Y'>: it's not found as __main__.Y",)
```
Should this be the expected behavior?  As demonstrated in `test_classdef.py`, one could conceivably pickle a badly named `namedtuple` by messing with the `__name__` (or `__qualname__`).  However, one shouldn't need to do that.  I believe that the PR for this issue will enable pickling of a badly named `namedtuple`... and is a good solution.

@panamantis: Thanks for the comment.  I believe it's a duplicate of this or #75 or #56.  Cases that we see failure to serialize in the presence of `super` has been documented in other issues.

First thing I noticed it that you are using a slightly out of date version of `pathos`, and possibly a slightly incompatible version of `dill`.  The best thing to do, for the moment (until I get the new release of `pathos` out), is to use the version of `pathos` from github.  The version of `pathos` you are using is 1.5 yrs old.  The version of `dill` may be old as well, as you should not be importing `ctypes` if it doesn't exist as a module.  Now, I don't know if `pypy` has a `ctypes` module… if it does, then I'll need to add a patch for that case… and I'll need to see what methods it comes with.

Honestly, I don't know whether `pathos` is compatible with `pypy`… but it might be.  Not sure about `dill` either.  Can you post the version of `dill` you are using?

If `pypy` has `ctypes`, then I need to make a small patch to `dill`, where if `pythonapi` is not found, then it also skips the `CellType` and special cases for `FileType`.  Then things should probably be fine.  Actually, I should be complete… Let me know if any of these throw an error:

    >>> import ctypes
    >>> ctypes.pythonapi.PyCell_New
    >>> ctypes.py_object
    >>> ctypes.Structure
    >>> ctypes.c_long 
    >>> ctypes.c_voidp
    >>> ctypes.c_int
    >>> ctypes.POINTER
    >>> ctypes.cast

`dill` uses these to support pickling file handles (in special cases) and also for pickling cell objects.

If the import of `dill` works after an appropriate `ctypes` patch, then `pathos` should also work.

for cell in column.cells:

Ok. I think there's something around here that addresses how to handle this in a pinch. It really *should* be in the core code, but it's quite complex as far as which "wrapper" elements one wants to see the text from and which should remain hidden (like revision marks for deleted items, etc.) and no one has picked it up yet.

The basic idea is this:

```python
from docx.text.run import Run

def iter_all_paragraph_runs(paragraph):
    # ---get the w:p lxml element from the paragraph---
    p = paragraph._element
    # ---use XPath to get all the w:r descendents, wherever they may be---
    rs = p.xpath('.//w:r')
    # ---generate a Run object for each w:r element, in document order---
    for r in rs:
        yield Run(r, paragraph)
```

This represents forward progress, and should fix your present situation, but note it may also return text you don't want, as mentioned above. If it does return deleted text, you might be able to fix that by accepting all changes on the document; I believe that removes the "here's what it was before you changed this bit" text.

Let us know how you go :)

for this to work, your current model must already have some fields with values matching a remote one with equivalent fields. you drop the CompositeForeignKey in your module and this key will retrive the remote instance like a foreignkey would. 

it is always better to use a django's ForeignKey to map to the primarykey of a remote model. this lib was created to work with legacy database with schema that had composite foreign key. don't create your own models with composite foreign key, it's aways  better to just create a ForeignKey.

My argument is that, If the CI tests are going to include pep8 requirements then they should run by default in the local test suite to avoid surprises.  pytest-pep8 uses pytest-cache and only runs pep8 against those files that were modified or untested previously.  You can also configure it via pytest.ini to allow certain exceptions and skip certain files.   

If my argument is not enough to sway the group, it is ok, but I thought I should bring up the subject.

@benjaoming 

> Does it work if POST data was submitted? Can it loose other types of instance data being stored?

It's a cache-busting URL-based refresh. So it'll lose any data _not_ encoded in the URL that was made anytime before or after the URL is loaded on browser. 

I think this behavior is expected, though. There are instances of requests being constructed based off of information being cached in the frontend. If the cache is out of date, and a request is made based on that faulty information, it'll likely result in a server-side error. In this case, we would want to refresh the page, rebuilding the state and cache.

> Does refreshing risk to loose the original error data?

Yes, at least on the frontend. The data error data is held in the store in the frontend, so if we want a record of the error it would be a matter of logging the request/response in a log or something.

Following up, it looks like one of the core issues here (as far as this library is concerned) is that you're not seeing the response in your logs. While I can't be absolutely sure due to limited information about your circumstances, this sounds very similar to a [known issue](https://bitbucket.org/jurko/suds/issues/32/logdebug-unicode-encoding-problem-in) with the suds-jurko library.

One feature usually included with suds is the ability to retrieve the last message sent and received. This _could_ have been useful for your purposes, but it was removed from suds-jurko. As another alternative, you can patch in a custom MessagePlugin to add similar functionality.

```
import googleads.adwords
import logging
import suds
from suds.plugin import MessagePlugin
from suds.client import Client


logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logging.getLogger('suds.transport').setLevel(logging.DEBUG)


def AddTrial(client):
  # Add trial here...
  pass


def main():
  PatchAdwordsClient()
  client = googleads.adwords.AdWordsClient.LoadFromStorage()

  AddTrial(client)

  # print the last message received.
  print client.plugin.last_received().str()


class MessageStoringPlugin(MessagePlugin):
  def __init__(self):
    self.last_sent_message = None
    self.last_received_message = None

  def sending(self, context):
    if context.envelope:
      self.last_sent_message = context.envelope

  def parsed(self, context):
    if context.reply:
      self.last_received_message = context.reply

  def last_sent(self):
    return self.last_sent_message

  def last_received(self):
    return self.last_received_message


def PatchAdwordsClient():
  googleads.adwords.AdWordsClient.__init__ = PatchedAdWordsClientInit
  googleads.adwords.AdWordsClient.GetService = PatchedAdWordsClientGetService


def PatchedAdWordsClientInit(
    self, developer_token, oauth2_client, user_agent,
    client_customer_id=None, validate_only=False, partial_failure=False,
    https_proxy=None, cache=None):
  self.developer_token = developer_token
  self.oauth2_client = oauth2_client
  self.oauth2_client.Refresh()
  self.user_agent = user_agent
  self.client_customer_id = client_customer_id
  self.validate_only = validate_only
  self.partial_failure = partial_failure
  self.https_proxy = https_proxy
  self.cache = cache
  self.plugin = MessageStoringPlugin()


def PatchedAdWordsClientGetService(self, service_name, version=sorted(googleads.adwords._SERVICE_MAP.keys())[-1],
               server=googleads.adwords._DEFAULT_ENDPOINT):
  if server[-1] == '/': server = server[:-1]
  try:
    proxy_option = None
    if self.https_proxy:
      proxy_option = {
          'https': self.https_proxy
      }

    client = suds.client.Client(
        self._SOAP_SERVICE_FORMAT %
        (server, googleads.adwords._SERVICE_MAP[version][service_name], version, service_name),
        proxy=proxy_option, cache=self.cache, timeout=3600, plugins=[self.plugin])
  except KeyError:
    if version in _SERVICE_MAP:
      raise googleads.errors.GoogleAdsValueError(
          'Unrecognized service for the AdWords API. Service given: %s '
          'Supported services: %s'
          % (service_name, _SERVICE_MAP[version].keys()))
    else:
      raise googleads.errors.GoogleAdsValueError(
          'Unrecognized version of the AdWords API. Version given: %s '
          'Supported versions: %s' % (version, _SERVICE_MAP.keys()))

  return googleads.common.SudsServiceProxy(
      client, googleads.adwords._AdWordsHeaderHandler(self, version))


if __name__ == '__main__':
  main()
```

Of course, this is just a work-around for now. We'll add another patch to the suds-jurko library in a future release that will fix this issue. We'll close this issue when we get around to it.

# FAQ

### How do I subset a font built with UFO source?

fontmake uses the [UFO lib.plist](http://unifiedfontobject.org/versions/ufo3/lib.plist/) source file to determine the glyphs to maintain within the subset font built from a given directory of UFO source.  This file is formatted as an XML property list and sits in the root of your source directory according to the UFO source specification.

The information that you need to create a list of glyphs that you intend to **maintain** in your compiled subset font include:

- glyph names (**Note**: not *.glif file paths)
- glyphsLib key string `com.schriftgestaltung.Keep Glyphs`

Glyph names can be identified as the keys in your [UFO source contents.plist file](http://unifiedfontobject.org/versions/ufo3/glyphs/contents.plist/) that is found in the glyphs directory of your source on the path `glyphs/contents.plist`.

Format the property list with the glyphsLib key string followed by an array of glyph name values that are formatted as string elements.

An example of a build subset that includes only the upper case A, B, and C glyphs in the compiled font is demonstrated below:

```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple Computer//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN"
"http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
  <key>com.schriftgestaltung.Keep Glyphs</key>
  <array>
    <string>A</string>
    <string>B</string>
    <string>C</string>
  </array>
</dict>
</plist>
```

Extend the array of glyph names to include all glyphs that you desire in your final font.

To build your subset font, execute fontmake with the `--subset` flag on your UFO source directory that includes the above lib.plist modifications as follows:

```
$ fontmake --subset -u Test-Regular.ufo
```

> However, I still don't think this is practical:

To be clear, all I'm claiming is that it's ~equally practical as the corresponding attack on browsers, so that *if* browsers need to use a secure RNG (i.e., *if* we believe that admonition in RFC 6455 at the top of this thread), then autobahn needs to as well.

> it is easy to trick a browser into opening an arbitrary, untrusted Web site, load untrusted JS from there - however, a Python client is trusted (by definition, as it can send arbitrary bytes anyway), and how do you trick the trusted Python client to connect to an untrusted server remotely?

I don't find this very reassuring. If I use `requests` to fetch a page from a remote server, then I don't generally expect that I'm letting that remote server take control over other random pages on other domains accessed by other programs. Similarly, if I use autobahn to talk to a remote server, that doesn't mean I trust that remote server with my credit card...

> one needs to observe 624 WebSocket frames first to derive the internal state of MT19937

Yes. This doesn't seem like a problem in practice, and is the same for both browsers and autobahn.

> because of WebSocket frame/message headers, one cannot control the bytes on wire by controlling the payload completely

Yes. This is the same for both browsers and autobahn.

> because of the 2+3, the intermediary being attacked would need to be broken in a very specific way

Yes. This is the same for both browsers and autobahn.

>> The mitigation is simple and easy: just use os.urandom
> No, it is too slow and drains entropy for important stuff.

As @meejah noted, "draining entropy" is [not a real thing](https://sockpuppet.org/blog/2014/02/25/safely-generate-random-numbers/). As for speed -- have you measured it?

Current code in autobahn:
```
$ pyperf timeit -s 'import struct, random' 'struct.pack("!I", random.getrandbits(32))'                       
.....................
Median +- std dev: 282 ns +- 6 ns
```

Code from [this gist](https://gist.github.com/njsmith/15c48e3e405bb6af73307196f94cb5b1):
```
$ pyperf timeit -s "from quickurandom import masking_keys; mk = masking_keys()" "next(mk)"        
.....................
Median +- std dev: 247 ns +- 6 ns
```

-> urandom can easily be made ~12% faster than what you're doing now. If it even matters. Is picking the masking key really a bottleneck as compared to, say, XORing the mask, or like, doing anything at all in Python? :-) Right now you're spending 2/3 of the time not in picking the random masking key, but just in Python data structure manipulation (`getrandbits` alone runs in ~110 ns).

> We can use B) when running over TLS and probably make it default. You can use A) if you believe it is necessary;)

I'm not bothered by choosing (B) over TLS as a default, though I guess it might make sense to double-check with Real Security Researchers :-). Presumably the WG had some reason that they didn't make this the default in the first place, and it might be useful to double-check what that was just in case.

But over non-TLS connections, then I think urandom really should be the default. As shown above, it doesn't have to be any slower, and "secure" is a *much* better default than "insecure" -- the people who should flip the "turn on security" switch generally are exactly the ones who don't realize that they should. Let the people who need to eke out every last bit of speed and are able to research the trade-offs be the ones who get to making active decision to flip the switch. And people generally expect a websocket implementation to have the security properties described in RFC 6455.

> I'd expect MT to be seeded from real entropy, yes.

Yes, Python's MT is seeded from urandom (unless someone explicitly sets the seed).

The main reason `to_number` defined `nulls` instead of type errors was so you could use it as a succinct way to filter out non-numeric values when combined with a projection.  For example:

```
>>> jmespath.search('sum([*].a.to_number(@))', [{"a": []}, {"a": "1"}, {"a": 2}, {"a": None}])
3
```

Those conditions don't really apply with `to_object` because it's not intended to be a generic conversion function.  It expects a specific structure in its input arguments, and will error out if it doesn't get the structure it expects.  That's why I'm hesitant for the name to suggest it's a generic function.  I feel something like `from_pairs` would clarify its expectations more explicitly.  What do you think?

Hi @vsoch, I think this one will be an uphill battle. A lot of it we can do; but very little of it is easy.
There's no obvious doc I can send you, so I'll just write things down as best I can.

> edit (something else I haven't found yet) to define a shared folder to be where the images are.

This is the only thing to which I have a "simple" answer -- sharing always needs to pass through a Shared Endpoint, even if it's hosted on a Globus Connect Personal endpoint.
So this is actually a callout to the Globus Transfer API to create a Shared Endpoint (rooted in whatever dir).

> The user has some (short) series of steps to set up the endpoint locally. Ideally, we could do this completely programatically (from within python) without needing to install "globuspersonalconnect" and "globus-cli" and still "globus-python-sdk" - that's a ton of dependencies for just this one thing!

I agree, this is a big hurdle for new users, though we're going to run into several issues trying to simplify.

Some initial reactions
- The `globus-sdk` is never a dependency end-users should see directly. Python programs can include it, and scripts which want to use it should distribute themselves with setuptools (so that they can depend on it). As long as the end-tool is packaged well, this shouldn't matter
- The `globus-cli` is about offering an interface to Globus APIs, and one of the things I love is that everything it does can be done via the SDK (so they dovetail nicely). I'm not _against_ it being integrated with Globus Connect Personal, but I'd have some pretty hefty demands for new/better Globus Connect Personal interfaces, and ideally SDK bindings for those capabilities...
- ...which brings us to Globus Connect Personal. It has a CLI (kinda) of its own under Linux, and different builds for each of the other major two platforms which don't include that CLI.
  - CLI and SDK are both fully cross platform products -- we promise that they work on Windows, Linux, and mac OS hosts. I think the biggest, and first, thing we'd need for CLI<=>Globus Connect Personal integration is a much better, and more uniform, way of controlling it and talking to it locally than disparate config files.
  - Best case, we'd have `globus_sdk.LocalGlobusConnectPersonal` or something which takes care of all of this stuff. Then, adding CLI commands for running it is easy.

I'm really wary of trying to interface with Globus Connect Personal as it currently exists. Across the three major platforms, it has significant differences, and hasn't been designed to be programmable.

> If I implemented it with the sregistry client

Mostly out of curiosity, is the sregistry client Python code?

Shipping `globus-sdk` (or even `globus-cli`) as a dependency is easy for Python. Embedding `globus-cli` to act as an interface for a non-Python language... although it would ideally work out, it's likely really hard.

> 1. The user creates the endpoint (or has sregistry do it for him/her) and adds the id to a variable that sregistry can access.

Okay, so there are actually multiple parts to this.

The first, creating an endpoint, itself consists of installing Globus Connect Personal and running the setup or starting the application. I think those steps are doable from your code if you're on Linux (and perhaps you don't care about the other platforms).
The setup command will consume a setup key -- you could direct users to the www.globus.org/app application for this. If you wanted to handle it for them, either the user needs to sign in (via Globus Auth) to an application which you control, or you need to have them `globus login` and then you can `globus endpoint create --personal ...` to get a setup key.

When you want to get the resulting endpoint ID back out, that's one of the few easy/doable things. We actually have an open issue [cli#371](https://github.com/globus/globus-cli/issues/371) for adding this functionality to the CLI (and maybe the SDK too). The issue describes where you could look for yourself for now.

> creating a specific share for the sregistry storage folder (if it doesn't exist)

I'm not sure how you'd do this 100% unambiguously, but there's probably a workable 80% solution if you do an Endpoint Search and filter down to shared endpoints with the desired Globus Connect Personal endpoint as a host endpoint.
See the [valid params to endpoint_search](https://docs.globus.org/api/transfer/endpoint_search/#endpoint_search) for more details.

> ensuring that the shared folder is by default not hidden (because by default it is hidden, and if I do a list on my command line I see nothing akin to the web interface.

This is a characteristic of every `ls` request made to the Transfer API -- there's a parameter for `show_hidden` which can be used to control whether or not dotfiles (those starting with `.`) are displayed.
In the CLI, it's a matter of `globus ls --all`, and in the web interface you need to select the menu option.

Wanting to create a folder whose name starts with `.`, but then wanting to ensure that it is visible, strikes me as very niche -- since it countervails the typical meaning and use of dotfiles.

Is there another solution open to you? Perhaps creating a symlink `singularity -> .signularity`?

They are not the same

- `TRINITY_DATA_DIR` -> The root directory that trinity will use for the various chain data like the IPC connection for JSON-RPC, the location of the chain database, etc.
- `TRINITY_ROOT_DIR` -> The root directory that trinity will use to put data directories for the different chains it runs.

By default:

- `TRINITY_ROOT_DIR` -> `~/.local/share/trinity`
- `TRINITY_DATA_DIR`  -> `$TRINITY_ROOT_DIR/<chain-name>`

> Would it be possible to set the priority for some channels to an equal value (e.g. defaults and conda-forge).

@janschulz, I'm frankly surprised that you would offer this one as an example. In fact, conda-forge is possible the single largest reason why we _need_ channel priority support. Why anyone would ever want to give them _equal priority_ with defautls is a mystery to me.

In fact, the conda-forge folks had to resort to artificially inflating their build numbers to ensure that their packages had higher priority than defaults. (This is not a criticism---they were doing the best they could with the old regime.) In other words, they didn't _want_ the two channels to have the same priority. Now, setting `channel_priority: True` and putting `conda-forge` ahead of `defaults` eliminates the need for them to play those games.

The two additions I would like to see are:
- `channel_priority: strict`: completely blocks packages from lower-priority channels from being considered.
- Per-package channel overrides.

OK, here's something interesting. I'm still not sure why the downgrade is happening, but I can explain why things differ depending on the value of `auto_update_conda`. 

The short answer is that the addition of the `conda` specs comes _after_ `add_defaults_to_specs`, and this needs to be reversed here. Here's why. If I type
```
conda install m2-file conda conda-env
```
then `add_defaults_to_specs` sees that `conda` and `conda-env` depend on Python, and it adds `python 3.6*` as an additional spec. However, if I do
```
conda install m2-file
```
then `add_defaults_to_specs`  sees that `m2-file` does _not_ depend on Python, so it doesn't think it needs to add anything to the specs. The problem with that is that then, somewhere _later_ in the process, you're adding `conda` and `conda-env` to the specs. So it _should_ have the exact same behavior as
```
conda install m2-file conda conda-env
```
The reason this bug usually doesn't come up is that frankly, most of the time, people are installing python-dependent packages with `conda install`. The behavior inconsistency is showing up only because `m2-file` is not a Python package.

So that's the explanation for the behavior difference. I consider this a bug that needs to be fixed, _independently_ of the mysterious downgrade behavior, which I am now going to investigate.

It's my understanding that `npm` changed its strategy for vendoring packages. The original intent of vendoring was to let each package use whatever version of a library it wanted, including libraries using libraries. Definitely an understandable goal. I'm probably using different words than npm uses to describe this.  It's been a while since I read about it.

OK, folks, sorry for the delay. Let's whittle down a simple example that will hopefully illustrate why it is problematic for packages to remain the the repository with broken metadata.

Consider packages `A`, `B`, and `C`
- `A-1.0-1`: depends on `B >=2`.
- `B-1.0-1`, `B-2.0-1`, `B-2.0-2`.
- `C-1.0-1`: depends on `B`, unversioned. However, when version 2 of package `B` was released, it was discovered to break `C`, so a new build was made:
- `C-1.0-2`: depends on `B <2`

Here are a number of ways that this package combination breaks.
1.`conda create -n env A C`.
2. `conda crate -n env C B>=2`.
3. In an existing environment containing `C-1.0-1` and `B-1.0-1`, the user learns about some important new features in `B`, so he does `conda update B`. Package `C` remains untouched, because conda does _not_ update packages unless it has to, in order to minimize disruption of user environments.
4. In another environment containing `C-1.0-1` and `B-1.0-1`, the user performs `conda update --all`. Conda is faced with a choice: bump version `B` up by 2 clicks, or bump version `C` up by 1 click. It chooses the latter because that's a "larger" upgrade.
5. In yet another environment containing `C-1.0-1` and `B-1.0-1`, the user decides he needs package A as well so he does `conda update A`. This forces `B` to upgrade, and `C-1.0-1` has no objection, so it proceeds unchallenged.

In all four of these cases, we're left with a broken environment, when what _should_ have happened was:
1,2. No environment is created due to dependency conflicts.
3. Package `B` is not updated, because doing so would conflict with the existing version of C.
4. Package `C` is updated, but package `B` is not.
5. The attempted installation of `A` would fail because of a conflict with C.

It's important to point out that in cases 3-5, the environment _was not broken before_. Sure, the metadata was wrong, in hindsight, but users _do not care_ about metadata when their environment is up and running; metadata matters only during installations, removals, and updates. And of course, we know this to be the case because the same environment with `C-1.0-2` and `B-1.0-1` would be functionally identical.

It's also important to note that these breakages occurred _even though_ the package developer for C was diligent about correcting his metadata and issuing a new build. _It wasn't enough_. Leaving he existing package in place ensures a variety of scenarios where the broken build will still be selected.

Again, to me there are _two_ different issues here:
1. Should the data in `repodata.json` be "hotfixed" to correct dependency issues like this?
2. Should these metadata changes cause a conda package to be _rebuilt_?
3. Should broken packages be deleted from the channel?
I do not feel strongly about 2. In fact, I've argued against it in other forums. In those same discussions, however, I've argued forcefully in favor of 1, and have even influenced conda-forge policy on the matter. (What about 3? Well,  it certainly prevents new installations of broken packages (scenario 1 above), but it doesn't help users with existing installations.)

Continuum deals with these kinds of reports quite often. Some are self-inflicted problems with packages we have built and served, and some involve packages from other channels, including conda-forge. I am fully confident that more people are well-served by hotfixing metadata than are poorly served by its other consequences.

However, the problem is that other scripts don't typically work.  For example `ipython` still fails, as you said above, because things like `ipython` don't get the message that the directory has changed.

Another (less-than-ideal) solution is to simply symbolically link the old directory to the new directory, rather than moving it.  Then, `ipython` still finds the right (old) directory, even though you're using the new name.  The only problem is that the old name still exists, and occupies that name's place.

What about icons (embedded into executables on Windows vs in an app bundle on macOS vs .desktop files on Linux) and Info.plist? Do you intend those to work with this? The stub executable cannot be made to be transparent on Windows due to how handles are managed by CreateProcess => this is due to the lack of fork, which you can use to achieve good transparency on the other OSes.

You should take a look at what I've done for RStudio for macOS (https://github.com/mingwandroid/conda-recipes/blob/master/rstudio/build.sh#L73-L80 and https://github.com/mingwandroid/conda-recipes/blob/master/rstudio/osx-post.sh) which is to have an .app bundle with an icon in $CONDA_PREFIX that has an executable which is simply a symlink to `$CONDA_PREFIX/bin/rstudio`. That functionality from the RStudio package should be moved into `menuinst` since that already does something similar for Windows.

IMHO this stuff is too OS dependent for a one-size-fits-all solution like I think you are proposing here. You'd get a lowest common denominator result from that.

Conda's version ordering closely follows the Python versioning standard https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/. It is not 100% identical (because conda must support other languages beyond Python), but is the best compromise I could come up with. 

Regarding your problem: an important recommendation is "version self-consistency". In your case, this means that the number of numeric version components should never change. So you should rename `2.1.1` into `2.1.1.0`. Then, appending a tag will never change the ordering.

You will get a Disconnected event if the server closes (gracefully or not), or if you call `close` on the websocket.

I've found that the network stack will not respond to pulling the ethernet cable for some time. And the exact behaviour seems to be platform dependant. Normally thats a good thing. You can pull an ethernet cable out and then plug it back in and nothing typically breaks.

If you want to be handle that case more precisely, you can do it at the application level. For instance if the server sends pings, then you could close the websocket if you haven't received a ping in  a while. Or you could send pings, and close if you don't receive the pong in time.

That is something that might be worthwhile adding to Lomond, an 'auto_ping' setting that auto-closes the websocket if it doesn't receive a timely response...

I have an experimental version of `VariantFile` that accepts filenames, file descriptors (integers), and file-like-objects that provide a `fileno()` method.  I don't yet support reading or writing from arbitrary Python file or file-like objects that don't supply a `fileno()` method to provide the underlying file descriptor.  You can grab it from [my pysam repo](https://github.com/bioinformed/pysam).

My test case is to take a `VariantFile` and pipe it through `subprocess.Popen` and stream it back to a new `VariantFile`.  Below is my test code (a bit rough, but good enough for a demo):

```python
import subprocess
import threading
import errno
from pysam import VariantFile

def writer_thread(infile, outfile):
    def _writer_thread(infile, outfile):
        try:
            i = 0
            for i, record in enumerate(infile, 1):
                outfile.write(record)
        except IOError as e:
            if e.errno != errno.EPIPE:
                raise
        finally:
            outfile.close()
            print('Wrote {} headers + {} records'.format(len(infile.header.records), i))

    writer = threading.Thread(target=_writer_thread, args=(infile, outfile))
    writer.daemon = True
    writer.start()
    return writer

proc = subprocess.Popen('head -n3000', stdin=subprocess.PIPE, stdout=subprocess.PIPE, shell=True)

in_vars  = VariantFile('test.vcf')
out_vars = VariantFile(proc.stdin, 'w', header=in_vars.header)
writer   = writer_thread(in_vars, out_vars)

with VariantFile(proc.stdout) as new_invars:
    i = 0
    for i, record in enumerate(new_invars, 1):
        pass
    print('Read {} headers + {} records'.format(len(new_invars.header.records), i))
```

I'm sorry you're all having a less than ideal experience. This issue should be completely obviated in the upcoming 4.0 release of Curator, by way of the following changes:

Index timestamps can be calculated in 3 different ways:

1. Timestring in the index name
2. Index creation time 
3. Using the Elasticsearch stats_api to look at the youngest and oldest documents in an index, based on a timestamp field.

Rather than calculate to a month, day, or hour, Curator will use epoch time for these 3 methods, and will normalize epoch + milliseconds (or any other higher resolution) down to just epoch seconds.  The calculation of what is older than, or younger than a given epoch time will be by way of a reference point, also calculated as epoch time.  There will no longer be any concept of "whole months," which is what led to this issue.  If an index is older or younger than the reference point in time, it will be acted upon.

In order to make this work for users, more filtering options will almost certainly be necessary.  As a result, Curator 4.0 will no longer have any command-line options other than a version output, but will instead use a YAML configuration file.

I hope to have a beta version of 4.0 out in the next few weeks.  Curator 4.0 will _not_ be reverse compatible with any version of Elasticsearch older than 2.0.  It will also _not_ work with AWS ES, as Curator requires access to the cluster metadata, which endpoint is blocked there.

Some overhead is to be expected when using Elastic APM, but I agree that ~400MByte seems excessive. 

One possible source for this could be internal caches that we use. The one with the largest impact is our source line cache, which is used when generating stack traces for errors and spans. Instead of reading the source file every time (which would cause a lot of I/O and be quite slow), we cache the result of the read:

https://github.com/elastic/apm-agent-python/blob/c8ae9e451ce216aac04cac477fd62d35f8ea98ef/elasticapm/utils/stacks.py#L30-L31

Could you try to disable capturing of stack traces for spans on one of your app servers and see if that helps? You can do that by setting [`span_frames_min_duration`](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/apm/agent/python/current/configuration.html#config-span-frames-min-duration-ms) to `0`.

Side bar -- for the results, we're actually modeled just as string in swagger. This was the swagger/autorest limitation -- giving this a variety of type possibilities. So we landed on just making all the row results string in swagger. The comments about timestamps apply to parameters, but not results. 

So, what we found with C# was actually that date time worked fine, but that we didn't have good support for ISO8601 intervals, which we use (e.g. query timespan). Do you happen to know if that would work well in Python? A cursory search tells me the standard datetime objects can do durations but not intervals. I haven't played around this with the SDK, which would answer my question...but I figured you would know best 😄 

If it ends up that the intervals/durations work well, I'll add a transform in the swagger to include format: duration/format: interval (or the appropriate keywords, per your guidance) to the swaggers for client generation. I think for ISO8601 interval in particular we didn't find a good "format" keyword to feed autorest to get the desired result.

For runtime (storage and servicebus), it's not currently exposed as a parameter to the service classes.

```
tableservice_object._httpclient.request_session = requests.Session()
```
Our plan is to use `requests` exclusively in the future.

OK I can understand that reasoning, and can see how it is useful. How about an alternate feature request, then: The ability to disallow *all* plugins from modifying files using a .pre-commit-config.yaml option.

One example of where automatic modification could cause problems is when the pre-push hook is used, because it seems to create a situation where a developer may run application tests using locally modified files and then end up pushing a different (modified) files to the server when `git push` is run. 

Sometimes the modifications made by the pre-commit hooks could have functional side-effects. Example: end-of-file-fixer may break files that are supposed to end with a non-newline. fix-encoding-pragma could break files that are dependent on system encoding. requirements-txt-fixer could break because the order of dependencies in requirements.txt is actually meaningful in cases where the pip packages underspecify dependencies. Fix-by-default also breaks any scenario where checksums are committed as part of a repository (such as you might use for a package manager or verifying the integrity of data files).
Yep, this approach was taken as hooks can sometimes be wrong and it allows the human some chance to double check the machine.

@Anvil  Do you mind clarifying the original scenario? 

Specifically, you say that Docker API returns data in large chunks. Let's say `aiohttp` actually did `yield` the entire chunk at a time. It seems that, even then, your `json.loads(chunk)` can fail. 

IIUC, the entire response is a valid JSON, so if you split it in 2 or more chunks, each of then chunks is guaranteed to be invalid JSON (if you split a JSON object or a JSON array into more than 1 piece, the `{`, `[` won't match the closing `}`, `]`).

So again IIUC, the your original code would only work correctly if Docker sent the entire content in a single chunk. But in that case, you might as well wait for the entire data to be received, there's no point iterating through chunks. 

(And in fact, Docker makes no guarantees that there's only 1 chunk sent, so you really should not iterate through chunks and attempt to `json.loads` each of them.)

See https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-dev.html#pending-task-destroyed

The debug messages are there for a reason. Closing the queue with tasks pending means that those tasks will never run. The implications of that depend on exactly what the tasks were supposed to do, but could include leaks, incorrect protocol behaviour, crashes, anything really. I only listed the warnings because they're a specific, predictable result, but the warnings are there to alert you to the less predictable and more serious potential consequences.

If you don't think the warnings are serious enough to warrant any effort to avoid them you could raise an issue suggesting they be removed, but I doubt it would get very far.

The success of the package now is a handicap. While the original purpose of the package was to supply a safe 1-off CA bundle, many packages have adopted this and as such, I can't easily instruct the packages to pick up an additional internal CA cert to be trusted. Modifying the packages / code to pick up 1-off is not possible a solution. One solution someone invented was to call `where()` and then amend the PEM ([link](https://incognitjoe.github.io/adding-certs-to-requests.html)) but that will fail if `certifi` is re-imported again at runtime. That RHEL forked the package to pick up the system default bundle shows that its useful and safe to do so.

Basically, consumers of 3rd party packages have no easy way to get that code to pick up internal CA certs without forking every package using certifi and changing the code where needed to use an internal certificate, as you're suggesting. Disappointing - an environment variable to use would be a safe improvement, not a security risk. Take the RHEL package as proof.

The best place to get started is the [tutorial](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/master/docs/tutorial.md).

To get the gradient with respect to all arguments at once, you can use the [multigrad function in convenience_wrappers.py](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/master/autograd/convenience_wrappers.py#L40-L51).

You probably don't want to use `from scipy import *` at the top of your file; you'll want to use the autograd versions of those functions. You could replace that line with `from autograd.scipy import *`, or you could just bind the module to a name with `import autograd.scipy as sp` and work from there.

Note, a workaround for this is to parse the IP out of the ref:

```python
# Input example:
# record:ptr/ZG5zLmJpbmRfcHRyJC4xLmFycGEuaW4tYWRkci4xOTIuMTM1LjIzMi4zMS5mcm9udGVuZDExLmFwb2NyaXRhLmhwYy5xbXVsLmFjLnVr:1.1.1.127.in-addr.arpa/Internal
ip = example.ref
ip = ip.split(':')[2]
ip = ip.split('.in-addr')[0]
# Reverse to real IP not PTR
ip = ip.split('.')
ip.reverse()
ip = ".".join(ip)

# Output example
# 127.1.1.1
```

> The reason I have a later version of bd2k-lib is because pip install s3am --pre pulls it.

Exactly. That's why we use virtualenv's. We need to isolate each program into its own virtualenv such that we can avoid conflicting dependency versions. Pip will happily ignore conflicting dependency versions and just chose one version arbitrarily. That approach will likely break all but one of the programs in the virtualenv.

So the solution is to use separate virtualenvs. One for s3am and one for cgcloud.

@nicoddemus I agree but that issue brought up the question (at least for me ) about how `pluggy` should support strict checking more generally. I do think that if you're looking at `pluggy` from a more "general purpose plugin system perspective" this kind of thing could be useful and maybe even moreso if you can configure "strictness" per hook?

I'm thinking mutiple cases in order of strictness:
- always require a `hookspec` for every loaded `hookimpl`
-  only require a spec after some dedicated call onwards (like `check_pending()` but instead enables the validation permanently after some point - `check_pending_forever()`)
- keep the single call check that `check_pending()` is
- allow for disabling validation - `disable_validation()`?

I also think `pending` is not clear and `validate()` or something similar is a better name.

Just some ideas.

```python
with pm.register(plugin):

The problem is actually in pytest-sugar. Just spotted this on the traceback:

```
INTERNALERROR>   File "/Users/jon/.virtualenvs/webapp/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pytest_sugar.py", line 217, in pytest_sessionstart
INTERNALERROR>     lines = self.config.hook.pytest_report_header(config=self.config)
```

`pytest_report_header` received a `startdir` parameter in [2.3.0](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/master/CHANGELOG.rst#230):

> pytest_report_header now receives a "startdir" so that you can use startdir.bestrelpath(yourpath) to show nice relative path

 and pytest-sugar doesn't [honor that](https://github.com/Frozenball/pytest-sugar/blob/master/pytest_sugar.py#L217).

It should be simple to reproduce it. Create two files in an empty directory:

```python
# conftest.py
def pytest_report_header(startdir):
    return 'hello: ' + startdir

# test_foo.py
def test():
    pass
```

And execute it with pytest-sugar active. It should give the exact same error and in fact I'm not sure `test_foo.py` is really needed to reproduce this.

Can you please confirm this? And if that's the case, report the issue at the [pytest-sugar repository](https://github.com/Frozenball/pytest-sugar/)?

To elaborate on this, I see four reasonable behaviours for managing the random numbers seed and state, namely
* use time as seed and in the end reset to the state on input (as implemented in the `Rotation` class only)
* use a given input seed and in the end reset to the state on input (as implemented in both classes)
* use the current state and in the end reset to the state on input, i.e. the same sequence is produced twice, inside and outside
* use the current state and do not reset in the end, i.e. advance the state (as implemented in the shift class only)

I believe this is because we rely on `django-phonenumber-field` to pull in the `phonenumbers` dependency, but it [does so dynamically], which runs into [problems because of how pip caches things](https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/3025).

This ideally needs to be fixed in `django-phonenumber-field` - the less ideal solution would be to make `phonenumbers` a direct dependency of Oscar.

In the mean time, you need to other clear your pip cache before installing, or just install `phonenumbers` manually.

@crackevil `normalized` just converts *fractional* well-defined quantities into the equivalent combination of integer sub-units, e.g. 0.5 days -> 12 hours, etc. There isn't a well-defined conversion from months->days, since "month" generally means something like "until the same day in the next calendar month", which depends on the left-hand-side of the equation, so `relativedelta(months=1)` and `relativedelta(days=30)` don't have a fixed ordering, since `(datetime(2017, 1, 15) + relativedelta(months=1)) > (datetime(2017, 1, 5) + relativedelta(days=30))`, but `(datetime(2017, 2, 15) + relativedelta(months=1)) < (datetime(2017, 2, 15) + relativedelta(days=30)`.

You are free to subclass `relativedelta` and implement `__lt__` and `__gt`__ as desired on the subclass if you want a specific ordering. Alternatively, if you don't care about the details and don't want to subclass, you could just add both relativedeltas to an arbitrary date (maybe the unix epoch `datetime(1970, 1, 1)`) and compare the results, like I did above. So long as the date is consistent, it should have more-or-less consistent results.

Now this request with a test case passes CI tests in AppVeyor!

### Important Note: 
The changes in this request seem to hit the platform-dependent behavior.
I guess it is a bug in Python but I failed to minimize the reproducible PoC code.

In this request, I add a workaround code in L76.
Once the problem is solved, this workaround becomes just a dead code and it can be safely removed from the code.

### Note about the problem I found:
- `re.match(item_regex, ' * ')` in comprehension in L69 in main.py does not work on Windows.
  - `item_regex` is compiled regex: `re.compile(r'^\s*\*\s+')`.
  - It works on Linux (Travis CI tester) and MacOSX (my local machine).
- Using none-compiled regex does not solve the problem.
- Using `filter`, `for` loop instead of comprehension does not solve the problem.
- I made the following code to try to reproduce the problem. However, it passes the CI tester in AppVeyor. I guess we need more complex code to reproduce it.
```python
    def test_regex(self):
        regex = re.compile(r'^\s*\*\s+')
        string = ' * '
        self.assertTrue(regex.match(string))
```

I am not sure if this is related but I did recently changed my python development environment on OS X and started using [pyenv][https://github.com/yyuu/pyenv] at which point I discovered that my shell noticeably slowed down when running `powerline`. After further investigation and testing I discovered that this is caused by `pyenv` versions of python: it creates shims for `powerline` commands. On OS X, one possible solution is to install a separate version of python via homebrew for example and ensure that all powerline commands are sourced from there instead.
Also make sure, that pyenv set the global python version to system:
```
pyenv global system
```
Please let me know if anyone else encountered this problem and could verify it. If so, this should be posted to pyenv and powerline issue tracking.

I agree with the premise of this issue.

For frame of reference, I wrote a script to compare the outputs of marshmallow, colander, and django-rest-framework:

```python
DATA = {'foo': 42, 'bar': 24}

### marshmallow ###

from marshmallow import Schema, fields

class MySchema(Schema):
    foo = fields.Str()
    bar = fields.Bool()

data, errors = MySchema().load(DATA)

print('--- marshmallow ---')
print(errors)

### colander ###

import colander as col

class MySchema(col.MappingSchema):
    foo = col.SchemaNode(col.String())
    bar = col.SchemaNode(col.Boolean())

print('--- colander ---')
try:
    MySchema().deserialize(DATA)
except col.Invalid as err:
    print(err.asdict())
except:
    pass

### DRF ###

from django.conf import settings
settings.configure(
    INSTALLED_APPS=(
        'rest_framework',
    )
)
import django
django.setup()

from rest_framework import serializers as ser


class MySer(ser.Serializer):

    foo = ser.CharField()
    bar = ser.BooleanField()


ser = MySer(data=DATA)
ser.is_valid()
print('--- DRF ---')
print(ser.errors)
```

Output

```
--- marshmallow ---
{}
--- colander ---
{'foo': "42 is not a string: {'foo': ''}"}
--- DRF ---
{'bar': ['"24" is not a valid boolean.']}
```

Strangely enough, colander considers numbers invalid input for String but valid input for Boolean.
DRF is the exact opposite: numbers are valid String input but invalid Boolean input.

When the inputs are changed to dicts or collections, colander and DRF both crash with `TypeError: unhashable type`.

As suggested by OP, numbers, dictionaries, and collections should probably not be considered valid input for String and Boolean fields.

I've started work on this in the [strictness](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/tree/strictness) branch. With these changes, the output of the script becomes:

```
--- marshmallow ---
{'foo': ['Not a valid string.'], 'bar': ['Not a valid boolean.']}
--- colander ---
{'foo': "42 is not a string: {'foo': ''}"}
--- DRF ---
{'bar': ['"24" is not a valid boolean.']}
```

I welcome any further feedback.

I had to revise my PR. My initial version used the same conversion that was specified by `JSON_API_FORMAT_KEYS`, but that didn't make any sense. If you have attribute `author_name` and you set conversion to `camelize` then you generate `authorName` in API responses. In the other direction you want to read requests that contain `authorName` and translate it back to Django's `author_name`.

So I've left original author's `underscore` as the only possible *request* translation format, but I only use it when `JSON_API_FORMAT_KEYS` is set (default is off).

In the logfile, multiple contexts are created and destroyed (and it ends in `Z3_del_context` for one of them). Is there any kind of concurrency involved, or are you just using multiple contexts to keep things separate?

No, Z3 does not keep a list of SMT2 commands, it only knows goals and solvers, which contain assertions. Push/pop as well as many other commands (e.g. set-options, tactics, etc) are executed immediately and not kept in any form of history. 

Further, note that `Z3_benchmark_to_smtlib_string` has been removed. This function produced SMT version 1.0 files, which Z3 does not support anymore. (There's a separate function for SMT 2.x.)

Oh, three more things I thought of:

- the doc string for `add_param()` needs updating to indicate that expressions are now also accepted (but I guess you were planning on getting to that anyway).

> Actually we could just use the as_theano_expression function in utils.

Nice idea. The only disadvantage would be that `(1 - as_theano_expression(beta2))` is a Theano expression even if beta2 is just a Python float, while `(floatX(1) - beta2)` becomes a Theano expression only if beta2 is a Theano expression. I.e., if beta2 is a Python float, `floatX(1) - beta2` will be computed while constructing the graph, and `(1 - as_theano_expression(beta2))` will become a graph that is only optimized via constant folding on compilation.
I'm undecided... it seems a bit hackish to replace some arguments by Theano expressions just to get Theano's upcasting rules instead of numpy's, but `one = floatX(1)` is not very elegant either. I think I'd be fine with both.

> Wouldn't be easier to just cast the whole update dict?

Easier to write maybe, but then the computation would take place in float64 (i.e., on the CPU!) and cast to float32 afterwards (i.e., copied back to GPU).

@lgeiger Tried that earlier, but happy to try again ;-)

## Problem

We need to get to parity with "classic" Jupyter with nteract's jupyter
extension. Right now, we really only have a component in core called
`NotebookApp` that handles the *editing* of a notebook.

## Jupyter Extension requirements

In our Jupyter extension, we can have the following cases:

Note that we don't necessarily know the `type` from the contents api ahead of
time. In this case, we may need to get the file and _then_ redirect.

^^ Is it possible to make a request for *just* the metadata about a file? I.e.,
not get the whole file? Probably not something that needs worrying about yet,
but it'd be wasteful to fetch the whole file and then just redirect.

```
/edit/<PATH> && { type: "notebook" }
/edit/<PATH> && { type: "file" }
/edit/<PATH> && { type: "directory" }

/view/<PATH> && { type: "notebook" }
/view/<PATH> && { type: "file" }
/view/<PATH> && { type: "directory" }

/tree/<PATH> && { type: "notebook" }
/tree/<PATH> && { type: "file" }
/tree/<PATH> && { type: "directory" }
```

## Plan

```
/edit/<PATH> && { type: "notebook" } >> <NotebookApp />
/edit/<PATH> && { type: "file" } >> ??
/edit/<PATH> && { type: "directory" } >> redirect(/tree/<PATH>)

/view/<PATH> && { type: "notebook" } >> redirect(/edit/<PATH>)
/view/<PATH> && { type: "file" } >> ??
/view/<PATH> && { type: "directory" } >> redirect(/tree/<PATH>)

/tree/<PATH> && { type: "notebook" } >> redirect(/view/<PATH>)
/tree/<PATH> && { type: "file" } >> redirect(/view/<PATH>)
/tree/<PATH> && { type: "directory" } >> <Directory />
```

Found something interesting!

'Add' and 'Sub' operations are handled in `_track`. The relevant code for 'Add':
```
[1]    sort, offset = temp_register_symbols[tmp]
[2]    offset += expr_1.ast.args[0]
       data_generated = (sort, offset)
```
Currently at [1] offsets can be negative and positive, lets assume offset is -4. The code does an (Add, 0xfffffff8), so at [2] offset becomes 0xfffffff4, instead of -0xc. The problem with this is that while both (sp+0xfffffff4 and sp-0xc) represent the same memory location, but two different SimMemoryVariables will be created for them.

Although I didn't see any problem arising from this, I think its still a logical error.

To demonstrate this, check the SimStackVariables with the original code, and with some correction:
Current code:
```
-0x8001004c set([<0x804840e 0x804840e(3)>])
-0x8001004b set([<0x804840e 0x804840e(3)>])
-0x8001004a set([<0x804840e 0x804840e(3)>])
-0x80010049 set([<0x804840e 0x804840e(3)>])
0x7ffeffb8 set([<0x80483ed 0x80483ed(5)>])
0x7ffeffb9 set([<0x80483ed 0x80483ed(5)>])
0x7ffeffba set([<0x80483ed 0x80483ed(5)>])
0x7ffeffbb set([<0x80483ed 0x80483ed(5)>])
``` 

Fixed up code:
```
0x7ffeffb4 set([<0x804840e 0x804840e(3)>])
0x7ffeffb5 set([<0x804840e 0x804840e(3)>])
0x7ffeffb6 set([<0x804840e 0x804840e(3)>])
0x7ffeffb7 set([<0x804840e 0x804840e(3)>])
0x7ffeffb8 set([<0x80483ed 0x80483ed(5)>])
0x7ffeffb9 set([<0x80483ed 0x80483ed(5)>])
0x7ffeffba set([<0x80483ed 0x80483ed(5)>])
0x7ffeffbb set([<0x80483ed 0x80483ed(5)>])
```

@mesmith75 I'm strongly leaning toward this being a ganga problem as the exception and the refusal to continue is thrown from within ganga with no bad information being returned by DIRAC.
The DIRAC devs asked if the API usage could be changed and there is different behaviour from the new `getReplicasForJobs` compared to the `getReplicas`. Ganga should take that into account if it's going to use the former method and should allow for jobs to be correctly split and submitted.

This problem can only occur if DIRAC is now reporting that these files have replicas but at no valid SE vs having no replicas when the SE is offline.
Combined with the statement of `Removing this LFN from the dataset!!` at https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/develop/ganga/GangaDirac/Lib/Splitters/OfflineGangaDiracSplitter.py#L223 this becomes more unexpected behaviour from the splitter in ganga and the fact that this can be fixed by users explicitly manually removing these files is proof of this.

Using the old API (Ganga 6.7.4) if DIRAC is reporting file replicas all of the time which are at invalid SE at the time of submission will lead to an increase in users seeing jobs stalling on the grid as their inputdata requirements cannot be met by the job matcher. This is potentially a change which comes from the running/version of DIRAC but either way my point that ganga isn't doing what it says correctly (it is removing the LFN from the dataset and then failing to report knowledge of this further into the splitting) holds, the test is correctly finding missing LFN lost in the splitting process and the exception thrown from ganga is highlighting this bug.

The phenomenon of the position of color and depth streams not matching up is described by RealSense stream programming expert JB455 as "an effect known as 'occlusion' - the depth and color cameras are in different physical positions, so can't see the all the same parts of the scene.  Hold something close to your face and open one eye at a time to understand this".

One way to gain better alignment of streams is to use a post-processing effect called a Decimation Filter.  This can increase or decrease the scale that a stream is displayed at.

https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/post-processing-filters.md#decimation-filter

If you need something temporarily, you can use this python script, it requires opencv and realsense python bindings. You could probably get past opencv by using another image library like Pillow (PIL). 

```
import argparse

import pyrealsense2 as rs
import numpy as np
import cv2
import os

def main():
    if not os.path.exists(args.directory):
        os.mkdir(args.directory)
    try:
        config = rs.config()
        if LOAD_BAG:
            rs.config.enable_device_from_file(config, args.input, False)
        pipeline = rs.pipeline()
        config.enable_stream(rs.stream.depth, 1280, 720, rs.format.z16, 30)
        pipeline.start(config)
        i = 0
        while True:
            print("Saving frame:", i)
            frames = pipeline.wait_for_frames()
            depth_frame = frames.get_depth_frame()
            depth_image = np.asanyarray(depth_frame.get_data())
            cv2.imwrite(args.directory + "/" + str(i).zfill(6) + ".png", depth_image)
            i += 1
    except RuntimeError:
        print("No more frames arrived, reached end of BAG file!")

    finally:
        pass


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("-d", "--directory", type=str, help="Path to save the images")
    parser.add_argument("-i", "--input", type=str, help="Bag file to read")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    main()
```

@shyuep Can you comment on this? It is at least related to custodian / scratch dir behavior and the solution might require custodian changes.

Brief summary of events:
1. When running a uniform band structure, atomate copies files from the static run (INCAR, POSCAR (via CONTCAR), POTCAR, KPOINTS, OUTCAR, vasprun.xml, CHGCAR) to the run directory. These files are unzipped (if zipped) and used to help set up the uniform job inputs by reading and modifying the static run outputs. 
2. Next, the uniform band structure inputs are created. Thus in the directory we now have:
- INCAR (for uniform run), POSCAR (for uniform run), KPOINTS (for uniform run), POTCAR (for uniform run), OUTCAR (for *static* run), vasprun.xml (for *static* run), CHGCAR (for *static* run). The last three are particularly important in this error.
3. Now that we have this directory set up, if we run custodian in the current directory there is no problem. The static run's CHGCAR, OUTCAR, and vasprun.xml are overwritten.
4. However, if we run custodian in this directory with scratch_dir and with zipped outputs, we have a problem. VASP runs in a scratch dir, all the files are zipped, and copied back. We now end up with the files list that Francesco mentioned. However, we have some duplicates - i.e., OUTCAR and OUTCAR.gz, with the former being from the static run and the latter from the uniform run. This causes downstream problems...

If it were just the vasprun.xml and OUTCAR, I would say to just fix this in atomate. e.g., after creating the inputs for the uniform run, clean the directory of any VASP output files in that directory. For implementation, we can either incorporate this as a step in existing FireTasks that write input files, or we can create a new FireTask that does this and should be run before custodian.

However, the CHGCAR makes things more tricky. I can't just delete the CHGCAR before running custodian because I need it for VASP. If I run custodian with a previous CHGCAR in that directory as a starting point with scratch_dir=True and gzip=True, then I end up with a directory that has both CHGCAR and CHGCAR.gz, with the former being the "old" calc and the latter being the "new" calc. This issue has nothing to do with atomate and would somehow need to be fixed in custodian or even monty. For example, when copying back the scratch directory back to the original directory, one might remove all the contents of the original directory (seems a bit unsafe...just one suggestion and certainly not a good default option) and then replace with the contents of the scratch directory. i.e., instead of the final directory being a union of the files in that were in the original directory and the scratch directory final outputs, the final directory state is the state of the scratch directory.

Here is a suggestion for the db approach.
### To check that issue no longer exists
 1. create and sync repo A, use any feed where errata exist, e.g. `fixtures/rpm-unsigned`
 1. make one of errata look the old, pulp 2.8.4, way (aka without `_pulp_repo_id`) and modify pkglist so it differs form the upstream one. Mongo commad to remove `_pulp_repo_id` and one package from pkglist: 
```
> db.units_erratum.update({"errata_id": "RHEA-2012:0055"},
                          {"$unset": {"pkglist.0._pulp_repo_id": ""},
                           "$pull": {"pkglist.0.packages": {"name": "walrus"}}})
```
 3. force sync of repo A, 2 times
 1. create and sync repo B using the same feed as for repo A
 1. create repo C and copy all the errata from repo B
 1. assert that in db in erratum pkglist:
 * there is still a collection without `_pulp_repo_id`
 * there is only one collection with `_pulp_repo_id == repo A`
 * there is one collection with `_pulp_repo_id == repo B`
 * there is no collection with `_pulp_repo_id == repo C`

### To check migration
 1. simulate the broken state for errata:
 * create and sync repo A, use any feed where errata exist, e.g. `fixtures/rpm-unsigned`
 * make multiple copies of collections in erratum
 ```
    > var sample_collection = db.units_erratum.find({"errata_id": "RHEA-2012:0055"})[0].pkglist[0]
    > for (i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
          db.units_erratum.update({"errata_id": "RHEA-2012:0055"},
                                  {"$push": {"pkglist": sample_collection}})
      }
 ```
 * modify collections to imitate an issue:
 -- collection with no `_pulp_repo_id`,
 -- multiple collections with the same name and same pkglits,
 -- last collection with the same name and different pkglist,
 -- at least one collection with different `__pulp_repo_id`.
```
    > var some_package = {"src": "http://smth.org", "name": "test", "sum": null, 
                          "filename": "test-1-1.noarch.rpm", "epoch": null,
                          "version": "1", "release": "1", "arch": "noarch"}
    > db.units_erratum.update({"errata_id": "RHEA-2012:0055"},
                              {"$unset": {"pkglist.0._pulp_repo_id": ""},
                               "$pull": {"pkglist.0.packages": {"name": "walrus"}},
                               "$set": {"pkglist.3._pulp_repo_id": "different_repo_id"},
                               "$push": {"pkglist.10.packages": some_package} })
```

 2. after running migration assert that in db:
 * there are 3 collections in pkglist
 * there is still pkglist without `_pulp_repo_id`
 * there is only one and the newest (includes `some_package` in my example) pkglist
 for `_pulp_repo_id == repo A`
 * there is a pkglist for `_pulp_repo_id == "different_repo_id"`


It is also worth checking that migration does not change/break anything in the normal non-broken state:
 * a case when there is only one collection in pkglist
 -- create and sync repo with some feed
 -- run migration
 -- assert that nothing changed in pkglist
 * a case when there are multiple collections in pkglist
-- create and sync 2 repos with the same feed (do not use copy)
-- run migration
-- assert that nothing changed in pkglist

def make_env(rank):
    def _thunk():
        env = base.make_env(env_id, process_idx=rank, outdir=logger.get_dir())

        if logger.get_dir():

        return env
    return _thunk

Do you mean you need to create the DatasourceType attributes from the result of another method call? If so then this should do the job:
```
def call_that_returns_fields_dict():
    result = {}
    for field in dynamicfields:
        result[field.name] = graphene.String()

    return result

DatasourceType = type('DatasourceType', (graphene.ObjectType,), call_that_returns_fields_dict())

```

If you mean to say you need to get the column names from your query as fields, then that means you will need to make the function above query the DB to find out the table's column names.

Btw, you should note that once you've created the schema, the fields will not and should not change. This is because the party querying your GraphQL API should not have the fields change on them at random per the spec. GraphQL only works when both the client and server agree on the schema.

Yeah I noticed the problems with removing a measurement in a Thellier experiment are much greater than in a Demag experiment. So here's the basic overview:

----------------------------

- Z or I step marked bad:
    - if there is another Z or I step of matching treatment use it instead and recalculate parameters (basically restarts a segment of the GUI, hope I didn't miss anything here because this is where things could go wrong) these are the IZZ, IIZ, ZZI, ZII cases where one of the duplicates is bad.
    - if there are no matching Z or I steps of the same treatment you have no choice but to throw out that treatment step and remove it from calculations even though there might be a different Z or I step still marked good. Data is reinitialized as if this treatment step is just non-existent. These are the ZI or IZ cases where one is bad. So basically how to handle only a single I or Z for a treatment step the solution being that there is no treatment step if you only have a single I or Z you need both so if there is one it just may as well not be there.
- PTRM checks, Tail checks, Additivity checks:
    - These are all just single data point measurements so I can just remove the bad points from the dataset and recalculate the parameters pertaining to differences between data points.
- NRM
    - Freak out and tell the user not to do this. Thellier GUI regularly accesses the first Z step as the NRM because that's where it puts it during data read in. This becomes a bit more troubling as later I need to keep that data but not treat it as the NRM to normalize things with. This also raises the problem of what do you do if the user marks all NRMs bad? I might look at this more later, but for now just implementing this for the other steps should be a large bonus for the users. By the way though I still let the user do this, but it does not prevent the NRM from being used for normalizing things and just removes it from PCA there is a really long warning message to tell the user this is kinda a bad idea. I can fully block it before merging if there are objections to this.

----------------------------

There are some troubles with this (mostly around the recalculation bits), but it is the most simplistic and intuitive approach I could think of that works without too much implementation mess. That said I might have missed some of the places where data is stored in Thellier GUI as it has data all over and such a major feature is doubtless to have fallout. Which is why I am hoping to test this rigorously on the separate live branch before merging. Also it would be good to have someone with better knowledge of Thellier GUI's data structures see if I caught all the places I need to recalculate after removing a point. This could mostly be done by checking the recalculation of statistics after marking things good or bad in the GUI and just looking at how the functions mark_meas_bad, mark_meas_good, and on_right_click_listctrl work none of which are long.

Interesting.

So, first a workaround: [SparkPost's transmissions API](https://developers.sparkpost.com/api/transmissions.html#transmissions-samples) has a `return_path` parameter for specifying the envelope sender. In Anymail, you can set that using `message.esp_extra = {'return_path': "mail@bounces.example.com"}`. (It's not currently documented in SparkPost's Python library, but it [seems to be](https://github.com/SparkPost/python-sparkpost/blob/v1.3.5/sparkpost/transmissions.py#L34) implemented.)

That said, I agree Anymail should try to handle this the same as Django's SMTP EmailBackend, at least for portability. (It's sadly not a particularly obvious way to control this, and doesn't seem to be documented anywhere outside that bug report.)

If I'm understanding correctly, the logic is, if `message.extra_headers["From"]` is set, then:
1. Use the extra_headers value as the From address (the Django code you linked above)
2. *And also* use `message.from_email` as the envelope sender (a.k.a. SMTP FROM). Django's code for this part is hiding out [in the SMTP EmailBackend](https://github.com/django/django/blob/a38ae914d89809aed6d79337b74a8b31b6d3849a/django/core/mail/backends/smtp.py#L122-L126).

The first part should be pretty straightforward to implement in Anymail's BasePayload. The second is ESP-specific: each ESP backend will need to pass envelope_sender to the API if supported:
* SparkPost has a `return_path` API param
* Mandrill has a `["message"]["return_path_domain"]` API param
* Mailjet has a `Sender` API param
* Mailgun requires the sender domain in its API URL (and Anymail actually invented [esp_extra sender_domain](https://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/stable/esps/mailgun/#email-sender-domain) to control this)
* Postmark doesn't allow specifying the Return-Path on an individual message; you can instead set up a custom Return-Path tied to the From email (or domain) at the time you validate it as a sender.
* SendGrid maybe doesn't support using a custom Return-Path? (Quick search didn't turn up any info.)

In any case, I think you can get the functionality you want by simply subclassing `LDAPAuthenticator` and overriding the existing `normalize_username` function: 
```python
    def normalize_username(self, username):
        """Normalize the given username and return it
        Override in subclasses if usernames need different normalization rules.
        The default attempts to lowercase the username and apply `username_map` if it is
        set.
        """
        username = username.lower()
        username = self.username_map.get(username, username)
        return username
```
https://github.com/jupyterhub/jupyterhub/blob/92088570eaf9a59e2b8e2ff692098cfd82f4ec4a/jupyterhub/auth.py#L197-L207

This can be done directly in your `jupyterhub_config.py` file - e.g.

```python
from ldapauthenticator import LDAPAuthenticator


class CustomLDAPAuthenticator(LDAPAuthenticator):
    def normalize_username(self, username):
        username = username.lower().split('@')[0]
        username = self.username_map.get(username, username)
        return username

c.JupyterHub.authenticator_class = CustomLDAPAuthenticator
```

Since I believe you can get the desired behaviour without changing `LDAPAuthenticator` itself I'll close this PR. If you disagree or think I've missed something feel free to re-open and we can continue the discussion...

-j is currently used to control the number of processes inside sos. For example if starting from several nodes if there are multiple roots of the DAG. It also controls the number of concurrent jobs (pool size) within a step. These two usages should share the same process pool as the number of processes can each `j*j` (#400).

Let us re-cap the three queues

1. execution paths of DAG (following different roots)
2. execution of concurrent tasks (for each execution path). Each step would wait for the completion of its jobs. Blocking one of the execution paths.
3. externally-executing jobs.

A proper solution would be making external taks really external.
